469	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1747-L1758).
470	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (Noether normalisation with denominators).}
471	  % NOTE: the denominator-clearing middle of this proof is a Finset-fold over the finite
472	  % generating set sigma, but each fold step is the Mathlib collapsing lemma
473	  % IsIntegral.exists_multiple_integral_of_isLocalization applied to the generator AS A
474	  % WHOLE (not to its individual coefficient polynomials via gf_clear_one_denominator).
475	  % For each generator x, that lemma yields a single a_x in A^0 (nonzero, since A is a
476	  % domain) with C(a_x) * (1 (x) x) integral over MvPolynomial A; set g1 := prod_x a_x and
477	  % g := g0 * g1. The AlgHom assembly below then runs over A_g exactly as before.
478	
479	  \emph{Step 1 — Noether normalisation over \(K\).} Apply Noether normalisation over the
480	  field \(K\) (\cref{lem:noether_normalization_fg}) to the finite-type \(K\)-algebra
481	  \(B_K = K \otimes_A B\): there are algebraically independent
482	  \(\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n \in B_K\) with \(B_K\) module-finite over
483	  \(K[\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n]\). Clearing denominators, each \(\bar b_j\) is
484	  represented by some \(b_j \in B\) (writing \(1 \otimes b\) as \(b\)).
485	
486	  \emph{Step 2 — clear the integral-dependence denominators to a single \(g\).} Fix a
487	  finite set \(\sigma\) of algebra generators of \(B_K\) over
488	  \(K[\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n]\). By Step 1 each generator's image
489	  \(1 \otimes x \in B_K\) is integral over \(K[\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n]\). For each
490	  \(x \in \sigma\) apply the Mathlib collapsing lemma
491	  \texttt{IsIntegral.exists\_multiple\_integral\_of\_isLocalization} (with
492	  \(R = \mathrm{MvPoly}\,A\), \(R_m = \mathrm{MvPoly}\,K\), \(S = B_K\), and the localizing
493	  submonoid the image of \(A^{0}\)): this returns a single
494	  \(a_x \in A^{0}\) (non-zero, as \(A\) is a domain) such that \(C(a_x)\cdot(1\otimes x)\)
495	  is integral over \(\mathrm{MvPoly}\,A = A[b_1, \dots, b_n]\). Multiplying over the finite
496	  set, set \(g_1 := \prod_{x \in \sigma} a_x\) and \(g := g_0 \cdot g_1\) (with \(g_0\) the
497	  Step-1 denominator witness). After inverting \(g\), every chosen generator becomes
498	  integral over \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\) and the generators generate \(B_g\) as an algebra;
499	  hence \(B_g\) is module-finite over \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\). (The single-coefficient
500	  lemma \cref{lem:gf_clear_one_denominator} provides the same denominator-clearing primitive
501	  at the level of one coefficient polynomial; the landed proof discharges a whole generator
502	  at once via the collapsing lemma, which folds that primitive over the coefficients
503	  internally.)
504	
505	  \emph{Step 3 — AlgHom assembly.} The algebra homomorphism \(\varphi\) of the statement
506	  is the \(A_g\)-algebra map \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n] \to B_g\) sending \(X_j \mapsto b_j\),
507	  where \(b_j \in B_g\) is the image of \(b_j \in B\). We establish injectivity and
508	  module-finiteness through a comparison with the situation over \(K\).
509	
510	  \emph{Step 3a — the comparison map \(\nu : B_g \to B_K\).} Since \(g \ne 0\) in the
511	  domain \(A\) and \(A \subseteq K\), the image of \(g\) is a unit of the field \(K\), so
512	  it is invertible in \(B_K = K \otimes_A B\). Hence the canonical \(A\)-algebra map
513	  \(B \to B_K\), \(b \mapsto 1 \otimes b\), sends \(g\) to a unit and, by the universal
514	  property of localisation, factors through a canonical \(A\)-algebra map
515	  \[
516	    \nu : B_g = B[g^{-1}] \longrightarrow B_K, \qquad \nu(b/g^k) = (1 \otimes b)\,(1 \otimes g)^{-k}.
517	  \]
518	  The map \(\nu\) is injective. Indeed, \(B\) is a domain, and both \(B_g\) and \(B_K\) are
519	  localisations of \(B\) at multiplicative subsets of the non-zerodivisors: \(B_g\) inverts
520	  the powers of \(g\), while \(B_K = K \otimes_A B\) inverts (in particular) the images of
521	  all non-zero elements of \(A\), a multiplicative set containing the powers of \(g\). A
522	  localisation of a domain at a set of non-zerodivisors is injective into any further such
523	  localisation. Concretely, the natural map \(B \to B_K\) is injective on the domain \(B\)
524	  (its kernel is the set of \(b\) annihilated by some non-zero element of \(A\), which in a
525	  domain over which \(A\) acts faithfully is \(0\)), so \(\nu(b/g^k) = 0\) forces
526	  \(1 \otimes b = 0\), hence \(b = 0\) and \(b/g^k = 0\). Under \(\nu\) the lifts
527	  \(b_j \in B_g\) of the Noether generators map to the elements \(\bar b_j \in B_K\) of
528	  Step 1: \(\nu(b_j) = \bar b_j\).
529	
530	  \emph{Step 3b — injectivity of \(\varphi\) by algebraic-independence descent
531	  \(K \to A_g\).} Injectivity of the \(A_g\)-algebra map
532	  \(\varphi : A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n] \to B_g\), \(X_j \mapsto b_j\), is equivalent to the
533	  algebraic independence of the family \((b_j)\) over \(A_g\): a non-zero element of the
534	  kernel is exactly a non-trivial \(A_g\)-polynomial relation \(P(b_1, \dots, b_n) = 0\).
535	  The localisation \(A_g = A[g^{-1}] \subseteq K\) embeds in the fraction field, giving an
536	  injection of \(A_g\)-algebras \(A_g \hookrightarrow K\). Now suppose
537	  \(P \in A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n]\) is non-zero with \(P(b_1, \dots, b_n) = 0\) in \(B_g\).
538	  Applying the injective map \(\nu\) and using \(\nu(b_j) = \bar b_j\) together with the fact
539	  that \(\nu\) is an \(A\)-algebra map extending scalars to \(K\), we obtain
540	  \(P^K(\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n) = 0\) in \(B_K\), where \(P^K\) is the image of \(P\)
541	  under the coefficient inclusion \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n] \hookrightarrow K[X_1, \dots, X_n]\).
542	  Since \(A_g \hookrightarrow K\) is injective, \(P \ne 0\) gives \(P^K \ne 0\); but the
543	  \(\bar b_j\) are algebraically independent over \(K\) (Step 1), so the only \(K\)-polynomial
544	  vanishing on them is \(0\), a contradiction. Hence no non-trivial relation exists, \((b_j)\)
545	  is algebraically independent over \(A_g\), and \(\varphi\) is injective.
546	
547	  \emph{Step 3c — module-finiteness of \(B_g\) over \(\operatorname{im}\varphi\).} By Step 1,
548	  \(B_K\) is module-finite over \(K[\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n]\); fix a finite set of algebra
549	  generators of \(B_K\) over \(K[\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n]\) (Step 2). Each such generator
550	  satisfies a monic equation of integral dependence whose coefficients are polynomials in
551	  \(K[\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n]\). The element \(g\) of Step 2 was chosen as a common
552	  denominator for the (finitely many) coefficient polynomials of these equations, so that
553	  after inverting \(g\) every such coefficient already lies in
554	  \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n] = \operatorname{im}\varphi\). Each chosen generator is the
555	  \(\nu\)-image of an element of \(B_g\), and its integral-dependence equation is the
556	  \(\nu\)-image of the corresponding monic equation holding in \(B_g\) with coefficients in
557	  \(\operatorname{im}\varphi\); since \(\nu\) is injective, that equation holds in \(B_g\)
558	  itself. Thus each generator of \(B_g\) is integral over the subalgebra
559	  \(\operatorname{im}\varphi\), and these generators generate \(B_g\) as an
560	  \(\operatorname{im}\varphi\)-algebra. A finite-type algebra that is integral over a
561	  subalgebra is module-finite over it; hence \(B_g\) is module-finite over
562	  \(\operatorname{im}\varphi = A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\). Transported along the injection
563	  \(\varphi\), this is exactly module-finiteness of \(B_g\) over \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n]\) for
564	  the algebra structure that \(\varphi\) puts on \(B_g\), as claimed. This recovers the
565	  source's conclusion that \(B_g\) is finite over \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\).
566	\end{proof}
567	
568	\begin{lemma}\leanok
569	[injectivity of a localisation lift]
570	  \label{lem:gf_isLocalization_lift_injective}
571	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.isLocalization_lift_injective}
572	  Let \(S\) be a localisation of a ring \(R\) at a multiplicative set \(M\), and let
573	  \(g : R \to P\) be a ring homomorphism sending every element of \(M\) to a unit of
574	  \(P\), so that it induces the lift \(\widehat g : S \to P\). If both the localisation
575	  map \(R \to S\) and \(g\) itself are injective, then \(\widehat g\) is injective.
576	\end{lemma}
577	\begin{proof}
578	  The lift is characterised by \(\widehat g(r/m) = g(r)\,g(m)^{-1}\). By the universal
579	  criterion for injectivity of a localisation lift, \(\widehat g\) is injective iff for
580	  all \(r_1, r_2 \in R\) the equality of localisation images
581	  \((r_1 \mapsto S) = (r_2 \mapsto S)\) implies \(g(r_1) = g(r_2)\). Both the hypothesis
582	  and the conclusion of that implication reduce, via injectivity of \(R \to S\) and of
583	  \(g\), to the single condition \(r_1 = r_2\); hence the implication holds and
584	  \(\widehat g\) is injective. (This helper is applied twice in
585	  \cref{lem:gf_noether_clear_denominators}, to the comparison maps \(\nu : B_g \to B_K\)
586	  and \(\psi : A_g \to K\).)
587	\end{proof}
588	
589	\begin{lemma}
590	
591	
592	\leanok
593	[L5a — the generic-rank short exact sequence]
594	  \label{lem:gf_generic_rank_ses}
595	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.gf_generic_rank_ses}
596	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, inductive step
597	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1760-L1765).
598	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
599	  % "Let $m$ be the generic rank of the finite module $B_g$ over the
600	  %  domain $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$. Then we have a short exact
601	  %  sequence of $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$-modules of the form
602	  %  $$0\to A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]^{\oplus m} \to B_g \to T \to 0$$
603	  %  where $T$ is a finite torsion module over $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$."
604	  % LEAN SIGNATURE (the scaffold creates the decl pinned above with this signature).
605	  %   Write P_d := MvPolynomial (Fin d) A.
606	  %     (A : Type*) [CommRing A] [IsDomain A] [IsNoetherianRing A]
607	  %       (d : ℕ) (N : Type*) [AddCommGroup N]
608	  %       [Module (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) N]
609	  %       [Module.Finite (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) N]
610	  %       [Module A N] [IsScalarTower A (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) N] :
611	  %       ∃ (m : ℕ) (φ : (Fin m → MvPolynomial (Fin d) A)
612	  %                        →ₗ[MvPolynomial (Fin d) A] N),
613	  %         Function.Injective φ ∧
614	  %         Module.IsTorsion (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) (N ⧸ LinearMap.range φ)
615	  %   witness: m = Module.finrank (FractionRing P_d)
616	  %                  (LocalizedModule (nonZeroDivisors P_d) N), the generic rank;
617	  %            φ = Fintype.linearCombination P_d v, with v : Fin m → N the
618	  %            denominator-cleared lifts of a FractionRing P_d-basis of the localisation.
619	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (generic-rank SES).}
620	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain, \(d \ge 0\), and \(N\) a finite module over the
621	  polynomial ring \(P_d := A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\) (with its induced \(A\)-module structure
622	  through the scalar tower \(A \to P_d \to N\)). Then there exist \(m \in \mathbb{N}\) and
623	  an injective \(P_d\)-linear map \(\varphi : P_d^{\oplus m} \to N\) whose cokernel
624	  \(T := N / \operatorname{im}\varphi\) is a torsion \(P_d\)-module. Equivalently, there is
625	  a short exact sequence of \(P_d\)-modules
626	  \[
627	    0 \to P_d^{\oplus m} \xrightarrow{\ \varphi\ } N \to T \to 0,
628	    \qquad T \text{ torsion}.
629	  \]
630	
631	  The integer \(m\) is the \emph{generic rank} of \(N\): the dimension of the
632	  \(\mathrm{Frac}(P_d)\)-vector space
633	  \(\mathrm{Frac}(P_d) \otimes_{P_d} N\), i.e.\ of the localisation of \(N\) at the
634	  non-zero-divisors of the domain \(P_d\). This step lives over \(P_d\) directly —
635	  \emph{no inversion of any element \(g \in A\) is required here}; the element \(g\) of
636	  Nitsure's argument enters only at the reindexing step \cref{lem:gf_torsion_reindex}.
637	\end{lemma}
638	
639	\begin{proof}
640	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1760-L1765).
641	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (generic-rank SES).}
642	  Write \(P_d = A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\); since \(A\) is a noetherian domain, so is \(P_d\)
643	  (Hilbert basis theorem, and a polynomial ring over a domain is a domain). Let
644	  \(Q = \mathrm{Frac}(P_d)\) be its fraction field and
645	  \(N_Q := Q \otimes_{P_d} N\) the localisation of \(N\) at the non-zero-divisors of
646	  \(P_d\); as \(N\) is finite over \(P_d\), \(N_Q\) is a finite-dimensional
647	  \(Q\)-vector space. Put \(m := \dim_Q N_Q\), the generic rank.
648	
649	  Choose a \(Q\)-basis of \(N_Q\) and clear denominators: each basis vector is a
650	  \(Q\)-unit multiple of the image of some \(v_i \in N\), so the images of
651	  \(v_1, \dots, v_m \in N\) again form a \(Q\)-basis of \(N_Q\). In particular the family
652	  \((v_i)_{i}\) is linearly independent over \(Q\); restricting scalars along the
653	  injection \(P_d \hookrightarrow Q\), it is linearly independent over \(P_d\). Let
654	  \(\varphi : P_d^{\oplus m} \to N\) be the \(P_d\)-linear map
655	  \((c_1, \dots, c_m) \mapsto \sum_i c_i v_i\). Linear independence of \((v_i)\) over
656	  \(P_d\) makes \(\varphi\) injective.
657	
658	  Set \(T := N / \operatorname{im}\varphi\). Because the images of \(v_1, \dots, v_m\)
659	  already span \(N_Q\) over \(Q\), the localised map \(\varphi \otimes Q : Q^{\oplus m}
660	  \to N_Q\) is an isomorphism, so \(Q \otimes_{P_d} T = 0\). A finite \(P_d\)-module whose
661	  localisation at the non-zero-divisors of \(P_d\) vanishes is torsion; hence \(T\) is a
662	  torsion \(P_d\)-module. This is the asserted short exact sequence.
663	\end{proof}

675	\begin{lemma}[L5b anchor — a finite torsion module has a non-zero-divisor annihilator]
676	  \label{lem:annihilator_meets_nonZeroDivisors}
677	  \lean{Submodule.annihilator_top_inter_nonZeroDivisors}
678	  \mathlibok
679	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, base case $n=-1$
680	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1724-L1727)
681	  % -- the same torsion-annihilation primitive, here provided in general by Mathlib.
682	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
683	  % "each $v\in M$ is annihilated by some non-zero element of $A$. Taking a finite
684	  %  generating set, and a common multiple of corresponding annihilating
685	  %  elements, we see there exists an $f\ne 0$ in $A$ with $fM=0$."
686	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
687	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring and \(M\) a finite torsion \(R\)-module. Then the
688	  annihilator of \(M\) meets the non-zero-divisors of \(R\): the set
689	  \((\top : \operatorname{Submodule} R\,M).\mathrm{annihilator} \cap R^{\circ}\) is
690	  non-empty, where \(R^{\circ}\) denotes the non-zero-divisors of \(R\).
691	\end{lemma}

693	\begin{lemma}\leanok
694	[L5b.1 — annihilator extraction for the torsion module]
695	  \label{lem:gf_torsion_annihilator}
696	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.gf_torsion_annihilator}
697	  \uses{lem:annihilator_meets_nonZeroDivisors}
698	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, base case $n=-1$
699	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1724-L1727).
700	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
701	  % "each $v\in M$ is annihilated by some non-zero element of $A$. Taking a finite
702	  %  generating set, and a common multiple of corresponding annihilating
703	  %  elements, we see there exists an $f\ne 0$ in $A$ with $fM=0$."
704	  % LEAN SIGNATURE (the scaffold creates the decl pinned above with this signature).
705	  %   Write P_d := MvPolynomial (Fin d) A. The conclusion is the existence of a
706	  %   non-zero annihilator; the downstream consequence "T is finite over P_d/(F)" is
707	  %   left to the assembly (a surjection P_d ↠ P_d/(F) pushes a finite generating set).
708	  %     (A : Type*) [CommRing A] [IsDomain A] [IsNoetherianRing A] (d : ℕ)
709	  %       (T : Type*) [AddCommGroup T] [Module (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) T]
710	  %       [Module.Finite (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) T]
711	  %       (htors : Module.IsTorsion (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) T) :
712	  %       ∃ F : MvPolynomial (Fin d) A, F ≠ 0 ∧
713	  %         F ∈ Module.annihilator (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) T
714	  %   (F ∈ annihilator unfolds to ∀ x : T, F • x = 0.)
715	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (torsion annihilation).}
716	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain, \(d \ge 0\), and \(T\) a finite \emph{torsion} module
717	  over \(P_d := A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\). Then there exists a non-zero
718	  \(F \in \operatorname{Ann}_{P_d}(T)\); consequently the \(P_d\)-action on \(T\) factors
719	  through \(P_d/(F)\) and \(T\) is a finite module over \(P_d/(F)\).
720	\end{lemma}
721	
722	\begin{proof}
723	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1724-L1727).
724	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (torsion annihilation).}
725	  The ring \(P_d = A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\) is a noetherian domain (a polynomial ring over the
726	  noetherian domain \(A\)), and \(T\) is a finite torsion \(P_d\)-module. By
727	  \cref{lem:annihilator_meets_nonZeroDivisors} the annihilator of \(T\) meets the
728	  non-zero-divisors of \(P_d\); choose \(F\) in that intersection. In the domain \(P_d\) a
729	  non-zero-divisor is exactly a non-zero element, so \(F \ne 0\), and \(F \cdot T = 0\).
730	  Since \(F\) annihilates \(T\), the \(P_d\)-module structure descends along the quotient
731	  map \(P_d \twoheadrightarrow P_d/(F)\); the image of any finite \(P_d\)-generating set of
732	  \(T\) generates \(T\) over \(P_d/(F)\), so \(T\) is finite over \(P_d/(F)\).
733	\end{proof}

735	\subsubsection{Nagata change-of-variables machinery}
736	\label{sec:gf_nagata_machinery}
737	
738	The Nagata normalisation lemma \cref{lem:gf_nagata_monic_lastVar} is assembled from
739	technical helper lemmas implementing the triangular change of variables and the
740	radix-encoding degree bookkeeping, following the Nagata trick of
741	\cite{nitsure-hilbert-quot}~\S4 over a noetherian domain. Throughout, \(k\) is a domain,
742	\(n \ge 0\), and \(f \in k[X_0, \dots, X_n]\) is a fixed polynomial; we write
743	\(\mathrm{up} := 2 + \deg_{\mathrm{tot}}(f)\) and the radix weights \(r_i := \mathrm{up}^{\,i}\)
744	for \(i = 0, \dots, n\) (so every exponent occurring in \(f\) is a base-\(\mathrm{up}\) digit).
745	The distinguished variable is \(X_0\).
746	
747	\begin{definition}\leanok
748	[Nagata substitution \(T_1\)]
749	  \label{def:gf_nagata_T1}
750	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.T1}
751	  \textit{Source: Nitsure \S4 (Nagata change of variables).}
752	  For a scalar \(c \in k\), the substitution \(T_1(c)\) is the \(k\)-algebra endomorphism
753	  of \(k[X_0, \dots, X_n]\) determined on generators by
754	  \[
755	    X_0 \mapsto X_0, \qquad X_i \mapsto X_i + c\,X_0^{\,r_i} \quad (i \ne 0).
756	  \]
757	  It is triangular in the variables \(X_i\) (\(i \ne 0\)) over the distinguished variable
758	  \(X_0\); this is the device realising the support-dimension drop.
759	\end{definition}
760	
761	\begin{lemma}\leanok
762	[\(T_1\) is invertible by the opposite shift]
763	  \label{lem:gf_t1_comp_t1_neg}
764	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.t1_comp_t1_neg}
765	  \uses{def:gf_nagata_T1}
766	  For every \(c \in k\), \(T_1(c) \circ T_1(-c) = \mathrm{id}\); the Nagata substitution is
767	  invertible, with inverse the substitution of opposite coefficient.
768	\end{lemma}
769	\begin{proof}
770	  \uses{def:gf_nagata_T1}
771	  It suffices to check the two composites agree on each generator: \(X_0 \mapsto X_0\), and
772	  \(X_i \mapsto X_i + (-c)X_0^{r_i} + c\,X_0^{r_i} = X_i\) for \(i \ne 0\).
773	\end{proof}
774	
775	\begin{definition}\leanok
776	[Nagata automorphism \(T\)]
777	  \label{def:gf_nagata_T}
778	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.T}
779	  \uses{def:gf_nagata_T1, lem:gf_t1_comp_t1_neg}
780	  \textit{Source: Nitsure \S4 (Nagata change of variables).}
781	  The Nagata change of variables is the \(k\)-algebra automorphism \(T := T_1(1)\) of
782	  \(k[X_0, \dots, X_n]\) — i.e.\ \(X_i \mapsto X_i + X_0^{\,r_i}\) (\(i \ne 0\)) and
783	  \(X_0 \mapsto X_0\) — packaged as an algebra equivalence with inverse \(T_1(-1)\) via
784	  \cref{lem:gf_t1_comp_t1_neg}.
785	\end{definition}
786	
787	\begin{lemma}\leanok
788	[Exponent bound is preserved by listing]
789	  \label{lem:gf_lt_up}
790	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.lt_up}
791	  If every entry of an exponent vector \(v\) satisfies \(v_i < \mathrm{up}\), then
792	  every entry of the list of values of \(v\) is also \(< \mathrm{up}\).
793	\end{lemma}
794	\begin{proof}
795	  The list of values of \(v\) consists exactly of the entries \(v_i\); each satisfies
796	  \(v_i < \mathrm{up}\) by hypothesis.
797	\end{proof}
798	
799	\begin{lemma}\leanok
800	[Injectivity of the radix encoding]
801	  \label{lem:gf_sum_r_mul_ne}
802	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.sum_r_mul_ne}
803	  \uses{lem:gf_lt_up}
804	  Let \(v, w\) be exponent vectors with all entries \(< \mathrm{up}\) and \(v \ne w\). Then
805	  the weighted base-\(\mathrm{up}\) sums differ:
806	  \(\sum_i r_i v_i \ne \sum_i r_i w_i\), where \(r_i = \mathrm{up}^{\,i}\).
807	\end{lemma}
808	\begin{proof}
809	  \uses{lem:gf_lt_up}
810	  The map \(v \mapsto \sum_i \mathrm{up}^{\,i} v_i\) is the value of the base-\(\mathrm{up}\)
811	  digit string \(v\); since every digit is \(< \mathrm{up}\) (\cref{lem:gf_lt_up}), this
812	  encoding is injective by uniqueness of base-\(\mathrm{up}\) expansions, so distinct
813	  \(v \ne w\) give distinct sums.
814	\end{proof}
815	
816	\begin{lemma}\leanok
817	[\(X_0\)-degree of a transformed monomial]
818	  \label{lem:gf_degreeOf_zero_t}
819	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.degreeOf_zero_t}
820	  \uses{def:gf_nagata_T}
821	  For a non-zero scalar \(a \ne 0\) and exponent vector \(v\), the degree in \(X_0\) of the
822	  transformed monomial is the radix value
823	  \[
824	    \deg_{X_0}\!\big(T(a\,X^v)\big) = \sum_i r_i v_i, \qquad r_i = \mathrm{up}^{\,i}.
825	  \]
826	\end{lemma}
827	\begin{proof}
828	  \uses{def:gf_nagata_T}
829	  Under \(T\), \(a\,X^v \mapsto a\,X_0^{v_0}\prod_{i \ne 0}(X_i + X_0^{r_i})^{v_i}\). Read as
830	  a polynomial in \(X_0\) over \(k[X_1, \dots, X_n]\), each factor
831	  \((X_i + X_0^{r_i})^{v_i}\) has \(X_0\)-degree \(r_i v_i\) and leading coefficient \(1\);
832	  the degrees add to \(\sum_i r_i v_i\) (the hypothesis \(a \ne 0\) keeps the top term from
833	  vanishing over the domain \(k\)).
834	\end{proof}
835	
836	\begin{lemma}\leanok
837	[Distinct monomials acquire distinct \(X_0\)-degrees]
838	  \label{lem:gf_degreeOf_t_ne_of_ne}
839	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.degreeOf_t_ne_of_ne}
840	  \uses{lem:gf_degreeOf_zero_t, lem:gf_sum_r_mul_ne}
841	  For distinct support exponents \(v \ne w\) of \(f\), the transformed monomials
842	  \(T(c_v X^v)\) and \(T(c_w X^w)\) (where \(c_v, c_w\) are the corresponding coefficients
843	  of \(f\)) have distinct degrees in \(X_0\), making the top \(X_0\)-degree of \(T(f)\) unique.
844	\end{lemma}
845	\begin{proof}
846	  \uses{lem:gf_degreeOf_zero_t, lem:gf_sum_r_mul_ne}
847	  By \cref{lem:gf_degreeOf_zero_t} the two degrees are \(\sum_i r_i v_i\) and
848	  \(\sum_i r_i w_i\). Each support exponent is bounded by the total degree of \(f\), hence
849	  \(< \mathrm{up} = 2 + \deg_{\mathrm{tot}} f\); radix-injectivity
850	  \cref{lem:gf_sum_r_mul_ne} then yields \(\sum_i r_i v_i \ne \sum_i r_i w_i\).
851	\end{proof}
852	
853	\begin{lemma}\leanok
854	[Leading coefficient of a transformed monomial]
855	  \label{lem:gf_leadingCoeff_finSuccEquiv_t}
856	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.leadingCoeff_finSuccEquiv_t}
857	  \uses{def:gf_nagata_T, def:gf_nagata_T1}
858	  For any exponent \(v\) with coefficient \(c_v := [X^v]\,f\), viewing \(T(c_v X^v)\) as a
859	  univariate polynomial in \(X_0\) over \(k[X_1, \dots, X_n]\), its leading coefficient in
860	  \(X_0\) is the constant \(c_v \in k\).
861	\end{lemma}
862	\begin{proof}
863	  \uses{def:gf_nagata_T, def:gf_nagata_T1}
864	  Each factor \((X_i + X_0^{r_i})^{v_i}\) is monic in \(X_0\) with leading coefficient
865	  \(1\); a product of monics is monic, so the leading coefficient of \(T(c_v X^v)\) in
866	  \(X_0\) is exactly the scalar \(c_v\).
867	\end{proof}
868	
869	\begin{lemma}\leanok
870	[Leading coefficient of \(T(f)\) is a non-zero constant]
871	  \label{lem:gf_T_leadingcoeff_eq}
872	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.T_leadingcoeff_eq}
873	  \uses{lem:gf_degreeOf_t_ne_of_ne, lem:gf_leadingCoeff_finSuccEquiv_t}
874	  Suppose \(f \ne 0\). Then there is a support exponent \(v \in \mathrm{supp}\,f\) such that
875	  the leading coefficient of \(T(f)\) in \(X_0\) equals the constant \(c_v := [X^v]\,f \ne 0\).
876	  Thus \(T(f)\) is, up to the non-zero scalar \(c_v\), monic in \(X_0\).
877	\end{lemma}
878	\begin{proof}
879	  \uses{lem:gf_degreeOf_t_ne_of_ne, lem:gf_leadingCoeff_finSuccEquiv_t}
880	  Expand \(f = \sum_{w \in \mathrm{supp}\,f} c_w X^w\). By \cref{lem:gf_degreeOf_t_ne_of_ne}
881	  the transformed monomials \(T(c_w X^w)\) have pairwise distinct \(X_0\)-degrees, so one of
882	  them — say at \(v\) — strictly dominates the others; the leading term of
883	  \(T(f) = \sum_w T(c_w X^w)\) is then that of \(T(c_v X^v)\), whose leading coefficient is
884	  the constant \(c_v\) by \cref{lem:gf_leadingCoeff_finSuccEquiv_t}. Since
885	  \(v \in \mathrm{supp}\,f\), \(c_v \ne 0\).
886	\end{proof}
887	
888	\begin{lemma}\leanok
889	[Naturality of the variable-splitting isomorphism under base change]
890	  \label{lem:gf_finSuccEquiv_map_comm}
891	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.finSuccEquiv_map_comm}
892	  For any ring homomorphism \(\varphi : A \to B\) and polynomial \(q \in A[X_0, \dots, X_m]\),
893	  the isomorphism \(A[X_0, \dots, X_m] \cong (A[X_1, \dots, X_m])[X_0]\) is natural with
894	  respect to applying \(\varphi\) coefficientwise.
895	\end{lemma}
896	\begin{proof}
897	  By induction on \(q\): the isomorphism is additive and multiplicative, and agrees with
898	  \(\varphi\) on constants and variables, so it commutes with \(\varphi\) on all polynomials.

1	\chapter{Section graded ring infrastructure: tensor powers and graded sections}
2	\label{chap:Picard_SectionGradedRing}
3	
4	% This chapter blueprints the Mathlib-absent infrastructure that the section
5	% graded ring R(X_s,L_s)=⊕_m Γ(X_s,L_s^{⊗m}) and section graded module
6	% M(X_s,F_s,L_s)=⊕_m Γ(X_s,F_s⊗L_s^{⊗m}) of Picard_QuotScheme.tex
7	% (def:sectionGradedRing / def:sectionGradedModule) depend on. Those two
8	% definitions are NOT restated here; this chapter supplies the three separable
9	% infrastructure layers they \uses{}. The target Lean file is
10	% AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/SectionGradedRing.lean (1:1 slug, to be scaffolded).
11	
12	\providecommand{\PshMod}{\mathrm{PresheafOfModules}}
13	\providecommand{\ShMod}{\mathrm{SheafOfModules}}
14	\providecommand{\Modules}{\mathrm{Modules}}
15	
16	The Hilbert-polynomial route of \cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme} represents the
17	degree-\(m\) graded component of a fibre by the global sections
18	\(\Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\) of a twist of a coherent
19	sheaf by a tensor power of a line bundle, and it organises these components into
20	a graded ring \(R(X_s, L_s) = \bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\)
21	(\cref{def:sectionGradedRing}) and a graded module
22	\(M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s) = \bigoplus_{m \ge 0}
23	\Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\)
24	(\cref{def:sectionGradedModule}). Three pieces of that picture have no direct
25	Mathlib counterpart at the pinned commit and must be built here, each in its own
26	section:
27	
28	\begin{enumerate}
29	  \item \textbf{Tensor powers of a sheaf of modules}
30	    (\cref{sec:sgr_tensor_powers}). Mathlib equips the category
31	    \(\PshMod\) of \emph{presheaves} of modules over a presheaf of commutative
32	    rings with a symmetric monoidal structure
33	    (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}) and provides the
34	    sheafification functor \(\PshMod \to \ShMod\)
35	    (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}); but the category
36	    \(\ShMod\) of \emph{sheaves} of modules carries \emph{no} monoidal
37	    structure. The tensor product of sheaves of modules
38	    \(\mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}\) must therefore be built
39	    as the sheafification of the pointwise tensor presheaf, and the iterated
40	    tensor power \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m}\) recursively from it.
41	  \item \textbf{Lax-monoidal global sections} (\cref{sec:sgr_lax_sections}).
42	    The global-sections functor \(\Gamma\) is only \emph{lax} monoidal: from the
43	    pointwise-strict monoidality of the presheaf tensor product and the
44	    sheafification unit one obtains a natural multiplication
45	    \(\Gamma(\mathcal{F}) \otimes_{\Gamma(\mathcal{O}_X)} \Gamma(\mathcal{G})
46	    \to \Gamma(\mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G})\), together with
47	    the associativity and unit coherence those products must satisfy.
48	  \item \textbf{Graded-ring / graded-module assembly}
49	    (\cref{sec:sgr_graded_assembly}). The section multiplications and the
50	    tensor-power comparison isomorphisms assemble, through Mathlib's
51	    external-direct-sum graded API (\cref{lem:directSum_gcommSemiring_mathlib},
52	    \cref{lem:directSum_gmodule_mathlib}), into the graded \emph{ring} structure
53	    on \(\bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\) and the graded
54	    \emph{module} structure on
55	    \(\bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\).
56	\end{enumerate}
57	
58	\section{Tensor powers of a sheaf of modules}
59	\label{sec:sgr_tensor_powers}
60	
61	We work over a fixed scheme \(X\) (in the application \(X = X_s\), a fibre over a
62	field \(\kappa(s)\)). Its category of sheaves of modules is
63	\(X.\Modules = \ShMod(\mathcal{O}_X)\), with global-sections notation
64	\(\Gamma(X, \mathcal{M}) = \mathcal{M}(X)\) for the value of the underlying
65	presheaf on the top open. The structure sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_X\) is a sheaf of
66	commutative rings, so its underlying presheaf factors through the category of
67	commutative rings; this is the hypothesis under which Mathlib's presheaf-level
68	monoidal structure applies.
69	
70	\begin{lemma}[Monoidal structure on presheaves of modules]
71	  \label{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}
72	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategory}
73	  \mathlibok
74	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
75	  (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Presheaf.Monoidal}).}
76	  Let \(R : \mathcal{C}^{\mathrm{op}} \to \mathrm{CommRingCat}\) be a presheaf of
77	  commutative rings. Then the category \(\PshMod(R)\) of presheaves of modules
78	  over \(R\) carries a symmetric monoidal structure whose tensor product is
79	  computed objectwise: for presheaves of modules \(\mathcal{M}_1, \mathcal{M}_2\)
80	  and an object \(U\),
81	  \[
82	    (\mathcal{M}_1 \otimes \mathcal{M}_2)(U)
83	      \;=\; \mathcal{M}_1(U) \otimes_{R(U)} \mathcal{M}_2(U),
84	  \]
85	  with restriction maps the tensor products of the restriction maps. The unit is
86	  the presheaf \(R\) viewed as a module over itself, and the associator,
87	  unitors, and braiding are induced objectwise from those of
88	  \(\mathrm{ModuleCat}\). In particular evaluation at a fixed object \(U\) is a
89	  \emph{strict} monoidal functor
90	  \(\PshMod(R) \to \mathrm{ModuleCat}(R(U))\).
91	\end{lemma}
92	
93	\begin{lemma}[Sheafification of presheaves of modules]
94	  \label{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
95	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.sheafification}
96	  \mathlibok
97	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
98	  (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Presheaf.Sheafification}).}
99	  Let \(R\) be a sheaf of (commutative) rings on a site, with underlying
100	  presheaf \(R_0\). There is a sheafification functor
101	  \[
102	    (-)^{\#} \;:\; \PshMod(R_0) \longrightarrow \ShMod(R),
103	  \]
104	  left adjoint to the forgetful functor, together with the unit
105	  \(\eta_{\mathcal{M}_0} : \mathcal{M}_0 \to (\mathcal{M}_0)^{\#}\) of the
106	  adjunction. On underlying sheaves of abelian groups it agrees with the
107	  ordinary sheafification, and it sends a presheaf of \(R_0\)-modules to the
108	  associated sheaf of \(R\)-modules.
109	\end{lemma}
110	
111	\begin{lemma}[The structure sheaf as the unit module]
112	  \label{lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}
113	  \lean{SheafOfModules.unit}
114	  \mathlibok
115	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
116	  (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Sheaf}).}
117	  For a sheaf of rings \(R\) (in the application \(R = \mathcal{O}_X\)) the
118	  structure sheaf, viewed as a module over itself, is a distinguished object
119	  \(\mathbf{1} = \mathcal{O}_X \in \ShMod(\mathcal{O}_X)\). It is the value at
120	  the zeroth tensor power, \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes 0} = \mathcal{O}_X\), and its
121	  global sections \(\Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X)\) form the degree-zero component of
122	  the section graded ring.
123	\end{lemma}
124	
125	\begin{definition}[Tensor product of sheaves of modules]
126	  \label{def:sheafTensorObj}
127	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj}
128	  \uses{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
129	    lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
130	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", \S "Tensor product" (Tag
131	  % 01CA), prose definition (read from references/stacks-modules.tex,
132	  % L2282-L2297).
133	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
134	  % "Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space and let
135	  %  $\mathcal{F}$ and $\mathcal{G}$ be $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules.
136	  %  We define first the {\it tensor product presheaf}
137	  %  $$
138	  %  \mathcal{F} \otimes_{p, \mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}
139	  %  $$
140	  %  as the rule which assigns to $U \subset X$ open the
141	  %  $\mathcal{O}_X(U)$-module
142	  %  $\mathcal{F}(U) \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)} \mathcal{G}(U)$.
143	  %  Having defined this we define the {\it tensor product sheaf}
144	  %  as the sheafification of the above:
145	  %  $$
146	  %  \mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}
147	  %  =
148	  %  (\mathcal{F} \otimes_{p, \mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G})^\#
149	  %  $$"
150	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", Tag 01CA (tensor
151	  product).}
152	  Let \(\mathcal{F}, \mathcal{G}\) be sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. Their
153	  \emph{tensor product} is the sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
154	  \[
155	    \mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}
156	      \;=\; \bigl(\mathcal{F} \otimes_{p,\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}\bigr)^{\#},
157	  \]
158	  the sheafification (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}) of the
159	  tensor product presheaf \(\mathcal{F} \otimes_{p,\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}\),
160	  whose value on an open \(U\) is the \(\mathcal{O}_X(U)\)-module
161	  \(\mathcal{F}(U) \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)} \mathcal{G}(U)\). Concretely, the
162	  tensor product presheaf is the Mathlib presheaf-level monoidal product
163	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}) of the underlying presheaves of
164	  modules of \(\mathcal{F}\) and \(\mathcal{G}\), and
165	  \(\mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}\) is its sheafification. This
166	  construction is symmetric and associative because the presheaf tensor product
167	  is, and sheafification preserves the coherence isomorphisms.
168	\end{definition}
169	
170	\begin{definition}[Tensor power of a line bundle]
171	  \label{def:sheafTensorPow}
172	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorPow}
173	  \uses{def:sheafTensorObj, lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}
174	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", \S "Invertible modules",
175	  % `definition-powers' (Tag 01CU) (read from references/stacks-modules.tex,
176	  % L4220-L4228).
177	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
178	  % "Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space. Given an invertible sheaf
179	  %  $\mathcal{L}$ on $X$ and $n \in \mathbf{Z}$ we define the
180	  %  $n$th {\it tensor power} $\mathcal{L}^{\otimes n}$ of $\mathcal{L}$
181	  %  as the image of $\mathcal{O}_X$ under applying the equivalence
182	  %  $\mathcal{F} \mapsto\mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{L}$
183	  %  exactly $n$ times."
184	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", Tag 01CU (tensor
185	  powers).}
186	  Let \(\mathcal{L}\) be a sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules (in the application
187	  an invertible sheaf / line bundle \(L_s\)). For \(m \in \mathbb{N}\) the
188	  \emph{\(m\)th tensor power} \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m}\) is defined by recursion
189	  on \(m\):
190	  \[
191	    \mathcal{L}^{\otimes 0} = \mathcal{O}_X
192	      \quad(\text{the unit module, \cref{lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}}),
193	    \qquad
194	    \mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m+1)}
195	      = \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{L}
196	        \quad(\cref{def:sheafTensorObj}).
197	  \]
198	  We restrict to non-negative powers, which is all the section graded ring uses;
199	  the Stacks definition extends \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes n}\) to all
200	  \(n \in \mathbf{Z}\) using invertibility, but negative powers are not needed
201	  here. For \(\mathcal{L}\) invertible each \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m}\) is again
202	  invertible.
203	\end{definition}
204	
205	\begin{definition}[Twist of a coherent sheaf by a tensor power]
206	  \label{def:sheafModuleTwist}
207	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.moduleTensorPow}
208	  \uses{def:sheafTensorObj, def:sheafTensorPow}
209	  Let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules (in the application
210	  a coherent sheaf \(\mathcal{F}_s\)) and \(\mathcal{L}\) a line bundle. For
211	  \(m \in \mathbb{N}\) the \emph{\(m\)-twist} of \(\mathcal{F}\) by
212	  \(\mathcal{L}\) is
213	  \[
214	    \mathcal{F}(m) \;:=\;
215	      \mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m}
216	      \qquad(\cref{def:sheafTensorObj}, \cref{def:sheafTensorPow}),
217	  \]
218	  the degree-\(m\) carrier of the section graded module. By construction
219	  \(\mathcal{F}(0) = \mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{O}_X \cong
220	  \mathcal{F}\).
221	\end{definition}
222	
223	\begin{lemma}[Tensor-power comparison isomorphism]
224	  \label{lem:sheafTensorPow_add}
225	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorPowAdd}
226	  \uses{def:sheafTensorPow, def:sheafTensorObj}
227	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", \S "Invertible modules"
228	  % (Tag 01CU, following `definition-powers') (read from
229	  % references/stacks-modules.tex, L4249-L4254).
230	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
231	  % "With this definition we have canonical isomorphisms
232	  %  $\mathcal{L}^{\otimes n} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X}
233	  %  \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \to
234	  %  \mathcal{L}^{\otimes n + m}$, and these isomorphisms
235	  %  satisfy a commutativity and an associativity constraint
236	  %  (formulation omitted)."
237	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", Tag 01CU (canonical
238	  power isomorphisms).}
239	  For a line bundle \(\mathcal{L}\) and \(m, m' \in \mathbb{N}\) there is a
240	  canonical isomorphism of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
241	  \[
242	    \mu_{m,m'} :
243	    \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}
244	    \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; \mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m+m')},
245	  \]
246	  natural in \(\mathcal{L}\), and these isomorphisms satisfy a commutativity
247	  constraint (\(\mu_{m,m'}\) agrees with \(\mu_{m',m}\) after the braiding) and
248	  an associativity constraint (the two ways of combining
249	  \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'} \otimes
250	  \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m''}\) into \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m+m'+m'')}\) agree).
251	\end{lemma}
252	
253	\begin{proof}
254	  \uses{def:sheafTensorPow, def:sheafTensorObj,
255	    lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}
256	  Fix \(m'\) and induct on \(m\). For \(m = 0\),
257	  \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes 0} \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'} =
258	  \mathcal{O}_X \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'} \cong \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}\)
259	  is the left unitor of the tensor product
260	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}, descended through
261	  sheafification). For the step, using the recursive definition
262	  \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m+1)} = \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes \mathcal{L}\)
263	  and the associator of the tensor product,
264	  \[
265	    \mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m+1)} \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}
266	    = (\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes \mathcal{L}) \otimes
267	      \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}
268	    \cong \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes
269	      (\mathcal{L} \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'})
270	    \cong \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes
271	      (\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'} \otimes \mathcal{L}),
272	  \]
273	  and the inductive comparison together with one more application of the
274	  recursive definition identifies this with
275	  \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m + m')} \otimes \mathcal{L} =
276	  \mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m + m' + 1)}\). The commutativity and associativity
277	  constraints follow from the corresponding coherence pentagon and hexagon of
278	  the symmetric monoidal structure on \(\PshMod\)
279	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}), which are preserved by the
280	  sheafification functor.
281	\end{proof}
282	
283	\section{Lax-monoidal global sections}
284	\label{sec:sgr_lax_sections}
285	
286	The graded multiplication is supplied by the lax-monoidal structure of the
287	global-sections functor \(\Gamma(X, -) : \ShMod(\mathcal{O}_X) \to
288	\mathrm{ModuleCat}(\Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X))\). It is only \emph{lax} because
289	global sections do not commute with sheafification: a global section of a
290	sheafified tensor product is not in general a finite sum of elementary tensors of
291	global sections.
292	
293	\begin{definition}[Section multiplication]
294	  \label{def:sectionMul}
295	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.sectionsMul}
296	  \uses{def:sheafTensorObj, lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
297	    lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
298	  Let \(\mathcal{F}, \mathcal{G}\) be sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. The
299	  \emph{section multiplication} is the \(\Gamma(X,\mathcal{O}_X)\)-bilinear map
300	  \[
301	    \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_{\Gamma(X,\mathcal{O}_X)}
302	      \Gamma(X, \mathcal{G})
303	    \;\longrightarrow\;
304	    \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G})
305	  \]
306	  defined as follows. A pair of global sections \((\sigma, \tau)\) determines,
307	  by the objectwise formula of \cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib} at the
308	  top open, the elementary tensor \(\sigma \otimes \tau \in
309	  (\mathcal{F} \otimes_{p,\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G})(X)\), a global section of
310	  the tensor product presheaf. Composing with the global-sections component of
311	  the sheafification unit
312	  \(\eta : \mathcal{F} \otimes_{p,\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G} \to
313	  (\mathcal{F} \otimes_{p,\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G})^{\#} =
314	  \mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}\)
315	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib},
316	  \cref{def:sheafTensorObj}) yields a global section
317	  \(\sigma \cdot \tau \in
318	  \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G})\). Bilinearity over
319	  \(\Gamma(X,\mathcal{O}_X)\) is inherited from bilinearity of the elementary
320	  tensor and \(\Gamma(X,\mathcal{O}_X)\)-linearity of \(\eta\).
321	\end{definition}
322	
323	\begin{lemma}[Coherence of the section multiplication]
324	  \label{lem:sectionMul_coherent}
325	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.sectionsMul_assoc_unit}
326	  \uses{def:sectionMul, lem:moduleUnit_mathlib, lem:sheafTensorPow_add}
327	  The section multiplications (\cref{def:sectionMul}) are associative and unital
328	  in the following sense. For sheaves \(\mathcal{F}, \mathcal{G}, \mathcal{H}\)
329	  the two composites
330	  \[
331	    \Gamma(\mathcal{F}) \otimes \Gamma(\mathcal{G}) \otimes \Gamma(\mathcal{H})
332	    \longrightarrow
333	    \Gamma\bigl((\mathcal{F} \otimes \mathcal{G}) \otimes \mathcal{H}\bigr)
334	    \cong
335	    \Gamma\bigl(\mathcal{F} \otimes (\mathcal{G} \otimes \mathcal{H})\bigr)
336	  \]
337	  agree after transport along the associator of \cref{def:sheafTensorObj}; and
338	  the unit element \(1 \in \Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X)\)
339	  (\cref{lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}) acts as a two-sided identity, i.e.\ the section
340	  multiplication by \(1\) is, after the unitor isomorphism
341	  \(\mathcal{O}_X \otimes \mathcal{F} \cong \mathcal{F}\), the identity on
342	  \(\Gamma(X, \mathcal{F})\).
343	\end{lemma}
344	
345	\begin{proof}
346	  \uses{def:sectionMul, lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
347	    lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
348	  Both statements reduce to the corresponding identities at the level of the
349	  tensor product presheaf, where by \cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}
350	  evaluation at the top open is strictly monoidal, so the associator and unitors
351	  act on elementary tensors by the usual formulas
352	  \((\sigma \otimes \tau) \otimes \upsilon \mapsto
353	  \sigma \otimes (\tau \otimes \upsilon)\) and
354	  \(1 \otimes \sigma \mapsto \sigma\). The sheafification unit \(\eta\) is a
355	  natural transformation, so it commutes with the associator and unitor
356	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}); applying \(\Gamma(X,-)\)
357	  to the naturality squares transports these presheaf-level identities to the
358	  section multiplications.
359	\end{proof}
360	
361	\section{Graded-ring and graded-module assembly}
362	\label{sec:sgr_graded_assembly}
363	
364	The components and multiplications of the previous two sections are assembled
365	into the graded ring and graded module through Mathlib's
366	\emph{external-direct-sum} graded API: the relevant inputs are families of
367	abelian groups indexed by \(\mathbb{N}\) together with multiplication maps
368	landing in the index-sum component, not submodules of a pre-existing ring.
369	
370	\begin{lemma}[External-direct-sum graded commutative semiring]
371	  \label{lem:directSum_gcommSemiring_mathlib}
372	  \lean{DirectSum.GCommSemiring}
373	  \mathlibok
374	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.DirectSum.Ring}).}
375	  Let \(A : \mathbb{N} \to \mathrm{Type}\) be a family of additive commutative
376	  monoids equipped with a graded multiplication
377	  \(A_i \times A_j \to A_{i+j}\) and a unit \(1 \in A_0\) satisfying graded
378	  associativity, two-sided unitality, distributivity, and graded commutativity
379	  (the data of a \(\mathrm{GCommSemiring}\) on \(A\)). Then the external direct
380	  sum \(\bigoplus_i A_i\) is a commutative semiring, with multiplication the
381	  bilinear extension of the graded multiplication and identity the image of
382	  \(1 \in A_0\).
383	\end{lemma}
384	
385	\begin{lemma}[External-direct-sum graded module]
386	  \label{lem:directSum_gmodule_mathlib}
387	  \lean{DirectSum.Gmodule}
388	  \mathlibok
389	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Module.GradedModule}).}
390	  Let \(A : \mathbb{N} \to \mathrm{Type}\) carry a graded semiring structure as
391	  in \cref{lem:directSum_gcommSemiring_mathlib} and let
392	  \(M : \mathbb{N} \to \mathrm{Type}\) be a family of additive commutative
393	  monoids with a graded scalar action \(A_i \times M_j \to M_{i+j}\) satisfying
394	  the graded module axioms (the data of a \(\mathrm{Gmodule}\) of \(M\) over
395	  \(A\)). Then the external direct sum \(\bigoplus_j M_j\) is a module over the
396	  semiring \(\bigoplus_i A_i\), with scalar multiplication the bilinear
397	  extension of the graded action.
398	\end{lemma}
399	
400	\begin{lemma}[Graded semiring structure on the section components]
401	  \label{lem:sectionGradedRing_gcommSemiring}
402	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.sectionGradedRing_gcommSemiring}
403	  \uses{def:sheafTensorPow, lem:sheafTensorPow_add, def:sectionMul,
404	    lem:sectionMul_coherent, lem:directSum_gcommSemiring_mathlib,
405	    lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}
406	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", \S "Invertible modules",
407	  % preceding `definition-gamma-star' (Tag 01CV) (read from
408	  % references/stacks-modules.tex, L4257-L4267).
409	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
410	  % "Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space. We can define a $\mathbf{Z}$-graded
411	  %  ring structure on $\bigoplus \Gamma(X, \mathcal{L}^{\otimes n})$ by mapping
412	  %  $s \in \Gamma(X, \mathcal{L}^{\otimes n})$ and
413	  %  $t \in \Gamma(X, \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m})$ to the
414	  %  section corresponding to $s \otimes t$ in
415	  %  $\Gamma(X, \mathcal{L}^{\otimes n + m})$.
416	  %  We omit the verification that this defines a commutative
417	  %  and associative ring with $1$."
418	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", Tag 01CV (the graded
419	  ring structure on \(\bigoplus \Gamma(X,\mathcal{L}^{\otimes n})\)).}
420	  Let \(L_s\) be a line bundle on \(X_s\). The family of
421	  \(\Gamma(X_s,\mathcal{O}_{X_s})\)-modules
422	  \(m \mapsto \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\) carries a graded commutative
423	  semiring structure (a \(\mathrm{GCommSemiring}\),
424	  \cref{lem:directSum_gcommSemiring_mathlib}) whose degree-\((m,m')\)
425	  multiplication is the composite
426	  \[
427	    \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m}) \otimes_{\Gamma(X_s,\mathcal{O}_{X_s})}
428	      \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m'})
429	    \xrightarrow{\ \cdot\ }
430	    \Gamma\bigl(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m} \otimes L_s^{\otimes m'}\bigr)
431	    \xrightarrow{\ \Gamma(\mu_{m,m'})\ }
432	    \Gamma\bigl(X_s, L_s^{\otimes (m+m')}\bigr),
433	  \]
434	  the section multiplication (\cref{def:sectionMul}) followed by the
435	  tensor-power comparison isomorphism (\cref{lem:sheafTensorPow_add}), with unit
436	  \(1 \in \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{O}_{X_s}) = \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes 0})\)
437	  (\cref{lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}). Consequently
438	  \(R(X_s, L_s) = \bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\) is a
439	  commutative semiring; this is the graded-ring structure underlying
440	  \cref{def:sectionGradedRing}.
441	\end{lemma}
442	
443	\begin{proof}
444	  \uses{lem:sheafTensorPow_add, lem:sectionMul_coherent,
445	    lem:directSum_gcommSemiring_mathlib}
446	  The graded multiplication maps are the composites displayed in the statement.
447	  Graded associativity is the associativity coherence of
448	  \cref{lem:sectionMul_coherent} combined with the associativity constraint of
449	  the comparison isomorphisms \(\mu\) (\cref{lem:sheafTensorPow_add}); graded
450	  commutativity is the unit/commutativity of the section multiplication combined
451	  with the commutativity constraint of \(\mu\); two-sided unitality is the unit
452	  coherence of \cref{lem:sectionMul_coherent} together with
453	  \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes 0} = \mathcal{O}_X\). These are exactly the axioms of a
454	  \(\mathrm{GCommSemiring}\), so \cref{lem:directSum_gcommSemiring_mathlib}
455	  endows \(\bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\) with its
456	  commutative-semiring structure.
457	\end{proof}
458	
459	\begin{lemma}[Graded module structure on the twisted-section components]
460	  \label{lem:sectionGradedModule_gmodule}
461	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.sectionGradedModule_gmodule}
462	  \uses{def:sheafModuleTwist, lem:sheafTensorPow_add, def:sectionMul,
463	    lem:sectionMul_coherent, lem:directSum_gmodule_mathlib,
464	    lem:sectionGradedRing_gcommSemiring}
465	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", \S "Invertible modules",
466	  % `definition-gamma-star' (Tag 01CV) (read from
467	  % references/stacks-modules.tex, L4279-L4286).
468	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
469	  % "Given a sheaf of $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules $\mathcal{F}$ we set
470	  %  $$
471	  %  \Gamma_*(X, \mathcal{L}, \mathcal{F})
472	  %  =
473	  %  \bigoplus\nolimits_{n \in \mathbf{Z}} \Gamma(X,
474	  %  \mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{L}^{\otimes n})
475	  %  $$
476	  %  which we think of as a graded $\Gamma_*(X, \mathcal{L})$-module."
477	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", Tag 01CV (the graded
478	  module \(\Gamma_*(X,\mathcal{L},\mathcal{F})\)).}
479	  Let \(\mathcal{F}_s\) be a coherent sheaf and \(L_s\) a line bundle on
480	  \(X_s\). The family
481	  \(m \mapsto \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\)
482	  (\cref{def:sheafModuleTwist}) carries a graded module structure (a
483	  \(\mathrm{Gmodule}\), \cref{lem:directSum_gmodule_mathlib}) over the graded
484	  semiring of \cref{lem:sectionGradedRing_gcommSemiring}, with degree-\((m,m')\)
485	  action
486	  \[
487	    \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m}) \otimes_{\Gamma(X_s,\mathcal{O}_{X_s})}
488	      \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m'})
489	    \xrightarrow{\ \cdot\ }
490	    \Gamma\bigl(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m} \otimes
491	      (\mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m'})\bigr)
492	    \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }
493	    \Gamma\bigl(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes (m+m')}\bigr),
494	  \]
495	  the section multiplication followed by the comparison isomorphism that
496	  reassociates and merges the line-bundle factors (using
497	  \cref{lem:sheafTensorPow_add} and the symmetry of the tensor product).
498	  Consequently \(M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s) = \bigoplus_{m \ge 0}
499	  \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\) is a graded module over
500	  \(R(X_s, L_s)\); this is the graded-module structure underlying
501	  \cref{def:sectionGradedModule}.
502	\end{lemma}
503	
504	\begin{proof}
505	  \uses{lem:sectionMul_coherent, lem:sheafTensorPow_add,
506	    lem:directSum_gmodule_mathlib, lem:sectionGradedRing_gcommSemiring}
507	  The action maps are the displayed composites. The graded-module axioms ---
508	  compatibility of the action with the ring multiplication
509	  (\(r \cdot (r' \cdot x) = (r r') \cdot x\)) and unitality
510	  (\(1 \cdot x = x\)) --- follow from the associativity and unit coherences of
511	  the section multiplication (\cref{lem:sectionMul_coherent}) together with the
512	  associativity and commutativity constraints of the comparison isomorphisms
513	  (\cref{lem:sheafTensorPow_add}), exactly as in the proof of
514	  \cref{lem:sectionGradedRing_gcommSemiring} but with the third tensor factor
515	  carrying \(\mathcal{F}_s\). These are the axioms of a \(\mathrm{Gmodule}\), so
516	  \cref{lem:directSum_gmodule_mathlib} produces the module structure of
517	  \(M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)\) over \(R(X_s, L_s)\).
518	\end{proof}

1	\chapter{Regrouping isomorphism for the affine base-change tensor tower}
2	\label{chap:Cohomology_RegroupHelper}
3	% This chapter blueprints the standalone, geometry-free helper that lives in
4	% AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/RegroupHelper.lean (1:1 slug). It is the pure
5	% tensor-algebra core of lem:base_change_mate_regroupEquiv in the FlatBaseChange
6	% chapter; it is split into its own compilation unit so the object-level
7	% ModuleCat iso can be obtained by a single LinearEquiv.toModuleIso application
8	% (the separate import normalises the `Module A (A ⊗[R] R')` instance diamond
9	% that blocks the same-file construction).
10	
11	\section{The R'-linear regrouping equivalence}
12	
13	\begin{lemma}
14	
15	\leanok
16	[Regrouping linear equivalence for the tensor tower]
17	  \label{lem:base_change_regroup_linearEquiv}
18	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.base_change_regroup_linearEquiv}
19	  \uses{lem:isPushout_cancelBaseChange_mathlib}
20	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma
21	  % `lemma-affine-base-change' (\S\,Cohomology and base change, I), proof, the
22	  % "boils down to the equality" step (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex,
23	  % L933--938).
24	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Thus we see that the lemma boils down to the
25	  % equality
26	  % $$
27	  % (R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M = R' \otimes_R M
28	  % $$
29	  % as $R'$-modules."
30	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma ``Affine base
31	  change'' (proof, ``boils down to the equality'' step).}
32	  Let \(\psi : R \to R'\) and \(\varphi : R \to A\) be ring homomorphisms and \(M\)
33	  an \(A\)-module. On the \emph{canonical} Mathlib instances (with \(R'\) acting on
34	  \(A \otimes_R R'\) through the \(\texttt{Algebra.TensorProduct.rightAlgebra}\)
35	  factor, i.e.\ \(r' \cdot (a \otimes s) = a \otimes (r' s)\)) there is a bundled
36	  \(R'\)-linear isomorphism
37	  \[
38	    \operatorname{regroup} :
39	    (A \otimes_R R') \otimes_A M \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; R' \otimes_R M ,
40	  \]
41	  the ``equality as \(R'\)-modules'' of the affine base-change source. On generators
42	  it sends \((a \otimes r') \otimes m \mapsto r' \otimes (a \cdot m)\); in
43	  particular \((1 \otimes r') \otimes m \mapsto r' \otimes m\), with inverse
44	  \(r' \otimes m \mapsto (1 \otimes r') \otimes m\). It carries \emph{no} flatness
45	  hypothesis.
46	\end{lemma}
47	
48	\begin{proof}
49	  \uses{lem:isPushout_cancelBaseChange_mathlib}
50	  \leanok
51	  This is pure tensor algebra, and it is supplied \emph{directly} by Mathlib's
52	  pushout-form base-change cancellation
53	  \(\operatorname{Algebra.IsPushout.cancelBaseChange}\)
54	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:isPushout_cancelBaseChange_mathlib}), instantiated at \(S = R'\),
55	  \(B = A \otimes_R R'\):
56	  \[
57	    \operatorname{cancelBaseChange} R\,R'\,A\,(A \otimes_R R')\,M :
58	    (A \otimes_R R') \otimes_A M \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; R' \otimes_R M .
59	  \]
60	  The required pushout instance \(\operatorname{Algebra.IsPushout} R\,R'\,A\,
61	  (A \otimes_R R')\) is supplied for free by \(\operatorname{TensorProduct.isPushout'}\).
62	  The decisive structural point is that this equivalence is \emph{natively}
63	  \(R'\)-linear: the cancellation is bundled as a \(\operatorname{LinearEquiv}\) over
64	  \(R'\) (its base-ring slot is \(S = R'\)), so the \(R'\)-linearity is part of the
65	  Mathlib datum with no hand-written scalar-compatibility obligation.
66	  On the generator it computes, by \(\texttt{cancelBaseChange\_tmul}\),
67	  \[
68	    (a \otimes r') \otimes m \;\longmapsto\; r' \otimes (a \cdot m) ,
69	  \]
70	  with inverse \(r' \otimes m \mapsto (1 \otimes r') \otimes m\)
71	  (\(\texttt{cancelBaseChange\_symm\_tmul}\)); the \(R'\)-action enters through the
72	  \(A \otimes_R R'\) factor (\(r' \cdot (a \otimes s) = a \otimes (r' s)\)). Being a
73	  single bundled Mathlib linear equivalence it is an \(R'\)-linear isomorphism, and it
74	  carries no flatness hypothesis.
75	
76	\end{proof}

1	\chapter{Relative Spec}
2	\label{chap:Picard_RelativeSpec}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/RelativeSpec.lean
4	
5	% STRATEGY NOTE (iter-172). This chapter is the foundational A.1.a sub-row of
6	% Route A (positive-genus arm of nonempty_jacobianWitness). It packages the
7	% relative-spectrum functor \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}) : \mathrm{QcohAlg}(X)^{op}
8	% \to \mathrm{Sch}/X used by the relative Picard functor on a product C \times_k T.
9	% Mathlib has the absolute Spec functor and affine gluing (Scheme.GlueData,
10	% AffineScheme.glueOpens) but no general RelativeSpec construction; this chapter is
11	% the prover-ready specification for the new file AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/RelativeSpec.lean
12	% which iter-173 (file-skeleton lane) will scaffold.
13	
14	\section{Setup and motivation}
15	\label{sec:relspec_setup}
16	
17	The Route~A construction of \(J = \Pic^0_{C/k}\) as a \(g\)-dimensional abelian variety is
18	organised around the relative Picard functor
19	\(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) = \Pic(C \times_k T) / \pi_T^* \Pic(T)\)
20	on the category of \(k\)-schemes. Representing \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) requires the line-bundle
21	pullback construction on a relative curve \(C \times_k T \to T\), which in turn rests on a
22	\emph{relative scheme} construction on a quasi-coherent sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras
23	\(\mathcal{A}\), the \emph{relative spectrum}
24	\(\pi : \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}) \to X\). This chapter packages the construction
25	of \(\underline{\Spec}_X(-)\) and its core properties --- universal property and affine
26	base --- as the foundational A.1.a building block. The relative Picard
27	functor itself (A.1.c), the line-bundle pullback functor on a product (A.1.b), and the
28	identity-component / dimension theory of \(J\) (A.3) live in separate sibling chapters
29	(\S~``Out of scope'' below).
30	
31	% SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Section ``Relative spectrum via glueing'' and ``Relative
32	% spectrum as a functor'' (tags 01LL, 01LO, 01LQ, 01LR, 01LS, available locally at
33	% references/stacks-constructions.tex L309-L720). The Hartshorne reference is
34	% [Hartshorne], II Exercise 5.17 (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf).
35	
36	\section{Quasi-coherent sheaves of algebras}
37	\label{sec:qc_sheaf_of_algebras}
38	
39	\begin{definition}\leanok
40	[Quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebra]
41	  \label{def:qc_sheaf_of_algebras}
42	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.QcohAlgebra}
43	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Section 01LL ``Relative spectrum via glueing'',
44	  % Situation ``situation-relative-spec''
45	  % (read from references/stacks-constructions.tex, L312-L318).
46	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
47	  % "Here \(S\) is a scheme, and \(\mathcal{A}\) is a quasi-coherent
48	  %  \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-algebra. This means that \(\mathcal{A}\) is a
49	  %  sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-algebras which is quasi-coherent as an
50	  %  \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-module."
51	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 01LL (situation-relative-spec).}
52	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. A \emph{quasi-coherent sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras} is a
53	  sheaf \(\mathcal{A}\) of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras (i.e. an \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module
54	  equipped with \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-linear multiplication and unit morphisms satisfying
55	  associativity, commutativity, and the unit law as sheaf morphisms) whose underlying
56	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module is quasi-coherent. We write \(\mathrm{QcohAlg}(X)\) for the
57	  category of quasi-coherent sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras and
58	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebra morphisms.
59	\end{definition}
60	
61	% NOTE (iter-179 review): supersedes the iter-174 carrier note. The iter-179
62	% Block A refactor (analogies/relative-spec-encoding.md) upgrades the carrier
63	% to a 3-field structure
64	%     `structure QcohAlgebra (X : Scheme.{u}) where`
65	%     `  sheaf        : TopCat.Sheaf CommRingCat.{u} X.toPresheafedSpace`
66	%     `  unit         : X.sheaf ⟶ sheaf`
67	%     `  coequifibered : NatTrans.Coequifibered`
68	%     `    (Functor.whiskerLeft (AffineZariskiSite.toOpensFunctor X).op unit.hom)`
69	% The new third field IS the iter-175+ overlay the prior note flagged: Mathlib
70	% commit `b80f227`'s Stacks-01LL `NatTrans.Coequifibered` predicate is strictly
71	% weaker than the full `SheafOfModules.IsQuasicoherent` claimed by Definition
72	% 1.1's prose but is equivalent for the geometric purpose (constructing the
73	% relative-spectrum scheme) via the dense-subsite equivalence
74	% `AffineZariskiSite.sheafEquiv`. This is the predicate Mathlib's
75	% `AffineZariskiSite.relativeGluingData` consumes, so the iter-179
76	% `RelativeSpec`/`structureMorphism` bodies (now real Mathlib values) close
77	% against it.
78	
79	\section{The relative-spectrum scheme}
80	\label{sec:relspec_scheme}
81	
82	The construction is given affine-locally: on every affine open \(U = \Spec R \subseteq X\) one
83	sets \(\pi^{-1}(U) := \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U))\) regarded as an \(R\)-algebra, and the local pieces
84	glue compatibly because \(\mathcal{A}\) is quasi-coherent (the transition isomorphism
85	\(\mathcal{A}(U) \otimes_R S \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{A}(V)\) for an inclusion
86	\(V = \Spec S \subseteq U\) gives an open immersion of the corresponding Specs).
87	
88	\begin{theorem}\leanok
89	[Relative spectrum exists]
90	  \label{thm:relative_spec_exists}
91	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RelativeSpec}
92	  \uses{def:qc_sheaf_of_algebras}
93	
94	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 01LQ (lemma-glue-relative-spec)
95	  % (read from references/stacks-constructions.tex, L381-L405).
96	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
97	  % "In Situation REF.
98	  %  There exists a morphism of schemes
99	  %  \(\)
100	  %  \pi : \underline{\Spec}_S(\mathcal{A}) \longrightarrow S
101	  %  \(\)
102	  %  with the following properties:
103	  %  \begin{enumerate}
104	  %  \item for every affine open \(U \subset S\) there exists an isomorphism
105	  %  \(i_U : \pi^{-1}(U) \to \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U))\) over \(U\), and
106	  %  \item for \(U \subset U' \subset S\) affine open the composition
107	  %  \(\)
108	  %  \xymatrix{
109	  %  \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U)) \ar[r]^{i_U^{-1}} &
110	  %  \pi^{-1}(U) \ar[rr]^{inclusion} & &
111	  %  \pi^{-1}(U') \ar[r]^{i_{U'}} &
112	  %  \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U'))
113	  %  }
114	  %  \(\)
115	  %  is the open immersion of Lemma REF above.
116	  %  \end{enumerate}
117	  %  Moreover, \(\underline{\Spec}_S(\mathcal{A})\)
118	  %  is unique up to unique isomorphism over \(S\)."
119	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 01LQ (lemma-glue-relative-spec);
120	  cf.\ [Hartshorne], II~Ex.~5.17(a).}
121	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(\mathcal{A}\) a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebra
122	  (\cref{def:qc_sheaf_of_algebras}). There exists a scheme
123	  \(\underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A})\) together with an affine morphism
124	  \(\pi : \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}) \to X\) such that
125	  \begin{enumerate}
126	    \item for every affine open \(U \subseteq X\), there is an isomorphism
127	      \(i_U : \pi^{-1}(U) \xrightarrow{\sim} \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U))\) over \(U\);
128	    \item for \(U \subseteq U' \subseteq X\) both affine open, the composite
129	      $\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U)) \xrightarrow{i_U^{-1}} \pi^{-1}(U) \hookrightarrow
130	      \pi^{-1}(U') \xrightarrow{i_{U'}} \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U'))$
131	      coincides with the open immersion induced by the restriction
132	      \(\mathcal{A}(U') \to \mathcal{A}(U)\).
133	  \end{enumerate}
134	  The pair \((\underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}), \pi)\) is unique up to unique isomorphism
135	  over \(X\).
136	\end{theorem}
137	
138	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Follows immediately from
139	% Lemmas REF,
140	% REF, and
141	% REF.
142	% Uniqueness is stated in the last sentence of
143	% Lemma REF."
144	\begin{proof}
145	  
146	  
147	\leanok
148	  By the open-cover gluing principle for schemes (Mathlib
149	  \texttt{Scheme.GlueData} / \texttt{AffineScheme.glueOpens}) it suffices to specify
150	  affine local pieces \(\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U))\) for each affine open \(U \subseteq X\) and
151	  open-immersion transition data
152	  \(\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U)) \hookrightarrow \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U'))\) for \(U \subseteq U'\)
153	  satisfying the cocycle condition. The transition morphisms come from the ring maps
154	  \(\mathcal{A}(U') \to \mathcal{A}(U)\); quasi-coherence yields the cartesian square
155	  \(\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U)) = U \times_{U'} \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U'))\), so each transition is an
156	  open immersion. Transitivity (Stacks lemma-transitive-spec) gives the cocycle condition.
157	  Uniqueness of the glued scheme is the standard property of the gluing functor.
158	\end{proof}

398	\section{Lean encoding}
399	\label{sec:relspec_lean_encoding}
400	
401	The Lean target file is the new \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/RelativeSpec.lean}.
402	The construction proceeds by gluing absolute affine pieces: for each affine open
403	\(U \subseteq X\) with \(U = \Spec R\) and \(A := \mathcal{A}(U)\) regarded as an
404	\(R\)-algebra, the local piece is \(\Spec A\) with structure morphism \(\Spec A \to \Spec R = U\)
405	induced by the algebra unit \(R \to A\). Mathlib provides \texttt{IsAffineOpen.toScheme} for
406	the affine-open inclusion and the absolute \texttt{Scheme.Spec} functor for the local
407	pieces; the transition isomorphisms come from quasi-coherence of \(\mathcal{A}\) via
408	\texttt{QuasiCoherent.tensorIso} (or the explicit
409	\(\mathcal{A}(U) \otimes_R S \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{A}(V)\) for \(V = \Spec S \subseteq U\)).
410	The gluing backbone is \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.GlueData} (general construction) or
411	the convenience wrapper \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.AffineScheme.glueOpens}; the cocycle
412	condition reduces to Stacks \texttt{lemma-transitive-spec}. The universal property
413	(\cref{thm:relative_spec_univ}) is expressed as a
414	\texttt{CategoryTheory.Functor.RepresentableBy} witness, providing the natural bijection
415	$\Hom_X(T, \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A})) \cong
416	\Hom_{\mathcal{O}_X\text{-alg}}(\mathcal{A}, g_*\mathcal{O}_T)$ as a
417	\texttt{Yoneda}-style structure. No new core axiom is required; the entire chapter is a
418	definitional/structural construction on top of existing Mathlib affine-scheme
419	infrastructure.
420	
421	\section{Out of scope}
422	\label{sec:relspec_out_of_scope}
423	
424	This chapter packages \emph{only} the relative-spectrum construction and its core
425	properties. The following downstream constructions live in separate sibling chapters and
426	are explicitly out of scope here:
427	\begin{itemize}

429	    defined-here; its blueprint home is the planned chapter
430	    \texttt{Picard\_RelPicFunctor.tex}.
431	  \item \textbf{Line-bundle pullback on a product \(C \times_k T\)} (A.1.b) ---
432	    \texttt{Picard\_LineBundlePullback.tex} (planned).
433	  \item \textbf{Connectedness, dimensionality, smoothness of \(\underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A})\)}
434	    --- downstream properties that depend on assumptions on \(\mathcal{A}\)
435	    (locally finite type, flat, etc.), not unconditional consequences of the construction.
436	  \item \textbf{Identity-component \(\Pic^0\) and abelian-variety structure} (A.3) --- a
437	    separate Route~A sub-row, fed by but not constructed in this chapter.
438	\end{itemize}
