1	\chapter{The Grassmannian over $\mathbb{Z}$: affine charts and gluing}
2	\label{chap:Picard_GrassmannianCells}
3	
4	\section{Set-up: charts indexed by $d$-subsets}
5	\label{sec:gr_setup}
6	
7	Fix integers \(r \ge d \ge 1\). For a \(d \times r\) matrix \(M\) over a ring
8	and a subset \(I \subseteq \{1, \dots, r\}\) with \(\#(I) = d\), write \(M_I\)
9	for the \emph{\(I\)-th minor} of \(M\): the \(d \times d\) submatrix whose
10	columns are those indexed by \(I\). We construct the Grassmannian scheme
11	\(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) over \(\mathbb{Z}\) by gluing one affine chart \(U^I\) for
12	each such \(I\). The construction is, conceptually, the quotient
13	\(\mathrm{GL}_{d,\mathbb{Z}} \backslash V\) of the scheme \(V\) of rank-\(d\)
14	matrices, but we never use the language of group-scheme actions: the charts and
15	their transition maps are elementary matrix algebra over \(\mathbb{Z}\). Taking
16	\(d = 1\) recovers \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,1) = \mathbb{P}^{r-1}_{\mathbb{Z}}\).
17	
18	\begin{definition}
19	
20	\leanok
21	[Affine chart \(U^I\)]
22	  \label{def:gr_affine_chart}
23	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.affineChart}
24	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
25	  % sub-section "Construction by gluing together affine patches" (read from
26	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L807-L821).
27	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
28	  % "If $M$ is a $d\times r$-matrix, and
29	  %  $I\subset \{1,\ldots, r\}$ with cardinality $\#(I)$ equal
30	  %  to $d$, the $I$ th minor $M_I$ of $M$ will
31	  %  mean the $d\times d$ minor of $M$ whose columns are indexed by
32	  %  $I$.
33	  %
34	  %  For any subset $I\subset \{1,\ldots, r\}$ with $\#(I) =d$,
35	  %  consider the $d\times r$ matrix $X^I$ whose $I$ the minor
36	  %  $X^I_I$ is the $d\times d$ identity matrix $1_{d\times d}$, while the remaining
37	  %  entries of $X^I$ are independent variables $x^I_{p,q}$ over $\Z$.
38	  %  Let $\Z[X^I]$ denote the polynomial ring in the variables
39	  %  $x^I_{p,q}$, and let $U^I = \spec \Z[X^I]$, which is non-canonically
40	  %  isomorphic to the affine space $\AA^{d(r-d)}_{\Z}$."
41	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
42	  Let \(I \subseteq \{1, \dots, r\}\) with \(\#(I) = d\). Let \(X^I\) denote the
43	  \(d \times r\) matrix whose \(I\)-th minor \(X^I_I\) is the \(d \times d\)
44	  identity matrix \(1_{d \times d}\) and whose remaining \(d(r - d)\) entries are
45	  independent indeterminates \(x^I_{p,q}\) (\(1 \le p \le d\),
46	  \(q \in \{1,\dots,r\} \setminus I\)) over \(\mathbb{Z}\). Write
47	  \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I]\) for the polynomial ring in these \(d(r-d)\) variables and
48	  set
49	  \[
50	    U^I \;:=\; \Spec \mathbb{Z}[X^I].
51	  \]
52	  The choice of an ordering of the free entries gives a (non-canonical)
53	  isomorphism \(U^I \cong \mathbb{A}^{d(r-d)}_{\mathbb{Z}}\); in particular
54	  \(U^I\) is a smooth affine \(\mathbb{Z}\)-scheme of relative dimension
55	  \(d(r-d)\). Intuitively a \(\mathbb{Z}\)-point of \(U^I\) is a \(d \times r\)
56	  matrix whose \(I\)-columns form the identity, i.e.\ a rank-\(d\) quotient of
57	  \(\mathbb{Z}^{r}\) trivialised on the coordinates \(I\).
58	\end{definition}
59	
60	\section{Transition maps and the cocycle condition}
61	\label{sec:gr_transition}
62	
63	\subsection*{Mathlib inputs for the transition map}
64	
65	The construction of \(\theta_{I,J}\) is a Cramer-inverse computation over the
66	localised chart ring, followed by one application of the universal property of a
67	localisation. We record the four Mathlib facts it rests on as dependency
68	anchors.
69	
70	\begin{lemma}
71	[Localised element is a unit]
72	  \label{lem:mathlib_away_algebraMap_isUnit}
73	  \lean{IsLocalization.Away.algebraMap_isUnit}
74	  \mathlibok
75	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
76	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, \(x \in R\), and \(S\) a localisation of \(R\)
77	  away from \(x\) (i.e.\ \(S = R[1/x]\)). Then the image of \(x\) under the
78	  structure map \(R \to S\) is a unit of \(S\).
79	\end{lemma}
80	
81	\begin{lemma}
82	[Left Cramer inverse]
83	  \label{lem:mathlib_nonsing_inv_mul}
84	  \lean{Matrix.nonsing_inv_mul}
85	  \mathlibok
86	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
87	  For a square matrix \(A\) over a commutative ring with \(\det A\) a unit, the
88	  nonsingular inverse \(A^{-1}\) satisfies \(A^{-1} A = 1\).
89	\end{lemma}
90	
91	\begin{lemma}
92	[Right Cramer inverse]
93	  \label{lem:mathlib_mul_nonsing_inv}
94	  \lean{Matrix.mul_nonsing_inv}
95	  \mathlibok
96	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
97	  For a square matrix \(A\) over a commutative ring with \(\det A\) a unit, the
98	  nonsingular inverse \(A^{-1}\) satisfies \(A A^{-1} = 1\).
99	\end{lemma}
100	
101	\begin{lemma}
102	[Universal property of an away-localisation]
103	  \label{lem:mathlib_away_lift}
104	  \lean{IsLocalization.Away.lift}
105	  \mathlibok
106	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
107	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, \(x \in R\), and \(S = R[1/x]\) a localisation
108	  of \(R\) away from \(x\). Given any ring homomorphism \(g : R \to P\) that
109	  sends \(x\) to a unit of \(P\), there is a unique ring homomorphism
110	  \(S \to P\) extending \(g\) along \(R \to S\).
111	\end{lemma}
112	
113	\subsection*{The transition map by matrix algebra}
114	
115	We now build \(\theta_{I,J}\) in seven steps over the localised chart ring
116	\(R^I_J := \mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J]\), splitting at the matrix-algebra seams. The
117	ambient chart ring \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I]\) is the polynomial ring of
118	\cref{def:gr_affine_chart} in the \(d(r-d)\) free variables \(x^I_{p,q}\)
119	(\(p \in \{1,\dots,d\}\), \(q \notin I\)).
120	
121	\begin{definition}
122	
123	\leanok
124	[The universal matrix \(X^I\)]
125	  \label{def:gr_universal_matrix}
126	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.universalMatrix}
127	  \uses{def:gr_affine_chart}
128	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
129	  % sub-section "Construction by gluing together affine patches" (read from
130	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L807-L821).
131	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
132	  % "For any subset $I\subset \{1,\ldots, r\}$ with $\#(I) =d$,
133	  %  consider the $d\times r$ matrix $X^I$ whose $I$ the minor
134	  %  $X^I_I$ is the $d\times d$ identity matrix $1_{d\times d}$, while the remaining
135	  %  entries of $X^I$ are independent variables $x^I_{p,q}$ over $\Z$."
136	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
137	  Let \(I \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\}\) with \(\#(I) = d\). The \emph{universal
138	  matrix} \(X^I\) is the \(d \times r\) matrix over the chart ring
139	  \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I]\) whose \(I\)-minor \(X^I_I\) is the \(d \times d\) identity
140	  \(1_{d\times d}\) and whose remaining entries are the free indeterminates
141	  \(x^I_{p,q}\) (\(1 \le p \le d\), \(q \notin I\)). It is the universal point of
142	  the chart \(U^I\) (\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}): a ring map
143	  \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I] \to A\) is the same as a \(d \times r\) matrix over \(A\)
144	  with identity \(I\)-minor.
145	\end{definition}
146	
147	\begin{definition}
148	
149	\leanok
150	[The minor determinant \(P^I_J\)]
151	  \label{def:gr_minor_det}
152	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.minorDet}
153	  \uses{def:gr_universal_matrix}
154	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Construction by gluing together
155	  % affine patches" (read from
156	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L822-L836).
157	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
158	  % "For any $J\subset \{1,\ldots, r\}$ with $\#(J) =d$, let
159	  %  $P^I_J = \det(X^I_J) \in \Z[X^I]$
160	  %  where $X^I_J$ is the $J$ th minor of $X^I$."
161	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
162	  For \(J \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\}\) with \(\#(J) = d\), let \(X^I_J\) denote the
163	  \(J\)-th minor of the universal matrix \(X^I\) (\cref{def:gr_universal_matrix})
164	  -- the \(d \times d\) submatrix whose columns are those indexed by \(J\). Set
165	  \[
166	    P^I_J \;:=\; \det\bigl(X^I_J\bigr) \;\in\; \mathbb{Z}[X^I].
167	  \]
168	  Then \(R^I_J := \mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J]\) (the localisation of the chart ring
169	  away from \(P^I_J\)) is the ring of the principal open subscheme
170	  \(U^I_J = \Spec \mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J] \subseteq U^I\) on which \(P^I_J\) is
171	  invertible. One has \(P^I_I = \det(1_{d\times d}) = 1\), so \(R^I_I =
172	  \mathbb{Z}[X^I]\) and \(U^I_I = U^I\).
173	\end{definition}
174	
175	\begin{definition}
176	
177	\leanok
178	[The localised \(J\)-minor \(X^I_J\) over \(R^I_J\)]
179	  \label{def:gr_universal_minor}
180	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.universalMinor}
181	  \uses{def:gr_universal_matrix, def:gr_minor_det}
182	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Construction by gluing together
183	  % affine patches" (read from
184	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L822-L836).
185	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
186	  % "Let $U^I_J = \spec \Z[X^I, 1/P^I_J]$
187	  %  the open subscheme of $U^I$ where $P^I_J$ is invertible."
188	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
189	  Write \(X^I_J \in \operatorname{Mat}_{d \times d}(R^I_J)\) for the \(J\)-minor of
190	  the universal matrix \(X^I\) (\cref{def:gr_universal_matrix}) with its entries
191	  pushed forward along the structure map \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I] \to R^I_J\) into the
192	  localised chart ring \(R^I_J = \mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J]\)
193	  (\cref{def:gr_minor_det}). By construction \(\det(X^I_J)\) is the image of
194	  \(P^I_J\) in \(R^I_J\).
195	\end{definition}
196	
197	\begin{lemma}
198	
199	\leanok
200	[The localised minor determinant is a unit]
201	  \label{lem:gr_minorDet_unit}
202	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.isUnit_det_universalMinor}
203	  \uses{def:gr_universal_minor, def:gr_minor_det, lem:mathlib_away_algebraMap_isUnit}
204	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Construction by gluing together
205	  % affine patches" (read from
206	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L822-L836).
207	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
208	  % "the open subscheme of $U^I$ where $P^I_J$ is invertible. This means
209	  %  the $d\times d$-matrix $X^I_J$ admits an inverse $(X^I_J)^{-1}$
210	  %  on $U^I_J$."
211	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
212	  The determinant of the localised \(J\)-minor \(X^I_J\)
213	  (\cref{def:gr_universal_minor}) is a unit of \(R^I_J\):
214	  \[
215	    \det\bigl(X^I_J\bigr) \in (R^I_J)^{\times}.
216	  \]
217	\end{lemma}
218	\begin{proof}
219	  \uses{def:gr_universal_minor, def:gr_minor_det, lem:mathlib_away_algebraMap_isUnit}
220	  \leanok
221	  By \cref{def:gr_universal_minor}, \(\det(X^I_J)\) is the image of \(P^I_J\)
222	  (\cref{def:gr_minor_det}) under the structure map
223	  \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I] \to R^I_J\). Since \(R^I_J\) is the localisation away from
224	  \(P^I_J\), that image is a unit by
225	  \cref{lem:mathlib_away_algebraMap_isUnit}.
226	\end{proof}
227	
228	\begin{definition}
229	
230	\leanok
231	[The Cramer inverse \((X^I_J)^{-1}\)]
232	  \label{def:gr_universalMinorInv}
233	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.universalMinorInv}
234	  \uses{def:gr_universal_minor}
235	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Construction by gluing together
236	  % affine patches" (read from
237	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L822-L836).
238	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
239	  % "the $d\times d$-matrix $X^I_J$ admits an inverse $(X^I_J)^{-1}$
240	  %  on $U^I_J$."
241	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
242	  Let \((X^I_J)^{-1} \in \operatorname{Mat}_{d \times d}(R^I_J)\) be the
243	  nonsingular (Cramer) inverse of the localised \(J\)-minor \(X^I_J\)
244	  (\cref{def:gr_universal_minor}). Its entries lie in \(R^I_J\) by Cramer's rule.
245	\end{definition}
246	
247	\begin{lemma}
248	
249	\leanok
250	[The Cramer inverse is a two-sided inverse]
251	  \label{lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities}
252	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.universalMinorInv_mul_cancel}
253	  \uses{def:gr_universalMinorInv, lem:gr_minorDet_unit, lem:mathlib_nonsing_inv_mul, lem:mathlib_mul_nonsing_inv}
254	  Because \(\det(X^I_J)\) is a unit of \(R^I_J\) (\cref{lem:gr_minorDet_unit}),
255	  the Cramer inverse \((X^I_J)^{-1}\) (\cref{def:gr_universalMinorInv}) is a
256	  genuine two-sided inverse:
257	  \[
258	    (X^I_J)^{-1}\, X^I_J = 1_{d\times d}
259	    \qquad\text{and}\qquad
260	    X^I_J\, (X^I_J)^{-1} = 1_{d\times d}.
261	  \]
262	\end{lemma}
263	\begin{proof}
264	  \uses{def:gr_universalMinorInv, lem:gr_minorDet_unit, lem:mathlib_nonsing_inv_mul, lem:mathlib_mul_nonsing_inv}
265	  \leanok
266	  Immediate from \cref{lem:gr_minorDet_unit} together with
267	  \cref{lem:mathlib_nonsing_inv_mul} (left identity) and
268	  \cref{lem:mathlib_mul_nonsing_inv} (right identity), both of which apply once
269	  \(\det(X^I_J)\) is known to be a unit.
270	\end{proof}
271	
272	\begin{definition}
273	
274	\leanok
275	[The image matrix \(M = (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\)]
276	  \label{def:gr_image_matrix}
277	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.imageMatrix}
278	  \uses{def:gr_universalMinorInv, def:gr_universal_matrix}
279	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Construction by gluing together
280	  % affine patches" (read from
281	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L822-L836).
282	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
283	  % "The images of the variables
284	  %  $x^J_{p,q}$ are given by the entries of the matrix formula
285	  %  $\theta_{I,J}(X^J) = (X^I_J) ^{-1}\,X^I$."
286	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
287	  Define the \(d \times r\) matrix over \(R^I_J\)
288	  \[
289	    M \;:=\; (X^I_J)^{-1}\, X^I \;\in\; \operatorname{Mat}_{d \times r}(R^I_J),
290	  \]
291	  the product of the Cramer inverse (\cref{def:gr_universalMinorInv}) with the
292	  universal matrix \(X^I\) (\cref{def:gr_universal_matrix}) base-changed to
293	  \(R^I_J\). Its entries are the prospective images of the indeterminates
294	  \(x^J_{p,q}\) under \(\theta_{I,J}\). Its \(J\)-minor is \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I_J =
295	  1_{d\times d}\) (\cref{lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities}), so \(M\) is a
296	  point of the chart \(U^J\); and its \(I\)-minor is \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I_I =
297	  (X^I_J)^{-1}\).
298	\end{definition}
299	
300	\begin{definition}
301	
302	\leanok
303	[The pre-localisation hom \(\tilde\theta_{I,J} : \mathbb{Z}[X^J] \to R^I_J\)]
304	  \label{def:gr_transition_pre}
305	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.transitionPreMap}
306	  \uses{def:gr_image_matrix}
307	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Construction by gluing together
308	  % affine patches" (read from
309	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L822-L836).
310	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
311	  % "a ring homomorphism
312	  %  $\theta_{I,J} : \Z[X^J, 1/P^J_I] \to \Z[X^I, 1/P^I_J]$ is
313	  %  defined as follows. The images of the variables
314	  %  $x^J_{p,q}$ are given by the entries of the matrix formula
315	  %  $\theta_{I,J}(X^J) = (X^I_J) ^{-1}\,X^I$."
316	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
317	  Let \(\tilde\theta_{I,J} : \mathbb{Z}[X^J] \to R^I_J\) be the
318	  \(\mathbb{Z}\)-algebra homomorphism out of the chart ring of \(J\) determined,
319	  via the universal property of the polynomial ring, by sending each free
320	  indeterminate \(x^J_{p,q}\) to the \((p,q)\)-entry of the image matrix \(M =
321	  (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\) (\cref{def:gr_image_matrix}); equivalently
322	  \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}(X^J) = (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\) as a matrix identity (the
323	  identity \(J\)-block of \(X^J\) matching the identity \(J\)-minor of \(M\)).
324	\end{definition}
325	
326	\begin{lemma}
327	
328	\leanok
329	[The pre-hom sends \(P^J_I\) to a unit]
330	  \label{lem:gr_transition_pre_unit}
331	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.isUnit_transitionPreMap_minorDet}
332	  \uses{def:gr_transition_pre, def:gr_minor_det, lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities, lem:gr_minorDet_unit}
333	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Construction by gluing together
334	  % affine patches" (read from
335	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L822-L836).
336	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
337	  % "In particular, we have $\theta_{I,J}(P^J_I) = 1/P^I_J$,
338	  %  so the map extends to $\Z[X^J, 1/P^J_I]$."
339	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
340	  The pre-localisation hom \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}\) (\cref{def:gr_transition_pre})
341	  sends \(P^J_I\) (\cref{def:gr_minor_det}, the \(I\)-minor determinant of
342	  \(X^J\)) to a unit of \(R^I_J\). Indeed
343	  \[
344	    \tilde\theta_{I,J}(P^J_I) = \det\bigl((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I_I\bigr)
345	      = \det\bigl((X^I_J)^{-1}\bigr) = \det(X^I_J)^{-1} = 1/P^I_J,
346	  \]
347	  which is a unit.
348	\end{lemma}
349	\begin{proof}
350	  \uses{def:gr_transition_pre, def:gr_minor_det, lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities, lem:gr_minorDet_unit}
351	  \leanok
352	  By \cref{def:gr_transition_pre}, \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}(X^J) = M = (X^I_J)^{-1}
353	  X^I\), so \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}\) carries the \(I\)-minor \(X^J_I\) of \(X^J\) to
354	  the \(I\)-minor of \(M\), which is \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I_I = (X^I_J)^{-1}\) since
355	  the \(I\)-minor of \(X^I\) is the identity. Hence \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}(P^J_I) =
356	  \det((X^I_J)^{-1}) = \det(X^I_J)^{-1}\). As \(\det(X^I_J)\) is a unit
357	  (\cref{lem:gr_minorDet_unit}), so is its inverse; the matrix-inverse
358	  determinant identity uses \cref{lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities}.
359	\end{proof}
360	
361	\begin{definition}
362	
363	\leanok
364	[Transition map \(\theta_{I,J}\)]
365	  \label{def:gr_transition}
366	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.transitionMap}
367	  \uses{def:gr_transition_pre, lem:gr_transition_pre_unit, lem:mathlib_away_lift, def:gr_affine_chart}
368	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Construction by gluing together
369	  % affine patches" (read from
370	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L822-L836).
371	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
372	  % "For any $I$ and $J$, a ring homomorphism
373	  %  $\theta_{I,J} : \Z[X^J, 1/P^J_I] \to \Z[X^I, 1/P^I_J]$ is
374	  %  defined as follows. ...
375	  %  In particular, we have $\theta_{I,J}(P^J_I) = 1/P^I_J$,
376	  %  so the map extends to $\Z[X^J, 1/P^J_I]$."
377	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
378	  Because the pre-localisation hom \(\tilde\theta_{I,J} : \mathbb{Z}[X^J] \to
379	  R^I_J\) (\cref{def:gr_transition_pre}) sends \(P^J_I\) to a unit of \(R^I_J\)
380	  (\cref{lem:gr_transition_pre_unit}), the universal property of the
381	  away-localisation (\cref{lem:mathlib_away_lift}) provides a unique ring
382	  homomorphism extending it along \(\mathbb{Z}[X^J] \to \mathbb{Z}[X^J, 1/P^J_I]\).
383	  This extension is the \emph{transition map}
384	  \[
385	    \theta_{I,J} : \mathbb{Z}[X^J, 1/P^J_I] \longrightarrow
386	    \mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J], \qquad \theta_{I,J}(X^J) = (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I .
387	  \]
388	  Dually \(\theta_{I,J}\) is the comorphism of a morphism of schemes
389	  \(U^I_J \to U^J_I\) (\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}), which we also denote
390	  \(\theta_{I,J}\); it sends the universal matrix \(X^I\) to \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\).
391	\end{definition}
392	
393	\begin{lemma}
394	
395	\leanok
396	[\(\theta_{I,I}\) is the identity]
397	  \label{lem:gr_transition_self}
398	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.transitionMap_self}
399	  \uses{def:gr_transition, def:gr_minor_det}
400	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Construction by gluing together
401	  % affine patches" (read from
402	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L838-L848).
403	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
404	  % "Note that $\theta_{I,I}$ is identity on $U^I_I = U^I$".
405	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
406	  For every \(I\), \(\theta_{I,I} = \id_{U^I}\): since \(P^I_I = 1\)
407	  (\cref{def:gr_minor_det}) the minor \(X^I_I = 1_{d\times d}\) is its own
408	  inverse, so \(\theta_{I,I}(X^I) = (X^I_I)^{-1} X^I = X^I\), i.e.\
409	  \(\theta_{I,I}\) (\cref{def:gr_transition}) is the identity ring homomorphism
410	  of \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_I] = \mathbb{Z}[X^I]\).
411	\end{lemma}
412	\begin{proof}
413	  \uses{def:gr_transition, def:gr_minor_det}
414	  \leanok
415	  When \(J = I\) one has \(P^I_I = \det(1_{d\times d}) = 1\)
416	  (\cref{def:gr_minor_det}), so \(R^I_I = \mathbb{Z}[X^I]\), the minor \(X^I_I\)
417	  is the identity, and its Cramer inverse is again the identity. Hence the image
418	  matrix is \(M = 1 \cdot X^I = X^I\), the pre-hom sends each \(x^I_{p,q}\) to
419	  itself, and its localisation lift (\cref{def:gr_transition}) is the identity.
420	\end{proof}
421	
422	\subsection*{Project-local matrix and minor bookkeeping}
423	
424	The cocycle computation rests on a handful of elementary matrix-algebra
425	identities over the (localised) chart rings. They are project-internal helpers
426	with no external source; each is verified directly in Lean.
427	
428	\begin{lemma}
429	
430	\leanok
431	[Column submatrix of a product]
432	  \label{lem:gr_mul_submatrix_col}
433	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.mul_submatrix_col}
434	  Let \(A \in \operatorname{Mat}_{d\times d}(R)\) and
435	  \(B \in \operatorname{Mat}_{d\times r}(R)\) be matrices over a commutative ring,
436	  and \(g : \{1,\dots,d\} \to \{1,\dots,r\}\) a column-index map. Then taking the
437	  column submatrix of the product equals multiplying by the column submatrix of
438	  the right factor:
439	  \[
440	    (A B)_{\bullet\, g} \;=\; A \,\bigl(B_{\bullet\, g}\bigr).
441	  \]
442	\end{lemma}
443	\begin{proof}
444	
445	\leanok
446	  Entrywise from the definition of matrix multiplication (reindexing the rows by
447	  the identity leaves the contraction index untouched). Proved directly in Lean.
448	\end{proof}
449	
450	\begin{lemma}
451	
452	\leanok
453	[A ring map commutes with the Cramer inverse]
454	  \label{lem:gr_map_nonsing_inv}
455	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.map_nonsing_inv}
456	  \uses{lem:mathlib_mul_nonsing_inv}
457	  Let \(f : R \to S\) be a homomorphism of commutative rings and
458	  \(A \in \operatorname{Mat}_{n\times n}(R)\) a square matrix with \(\det A\) a
459	  unit. Writing \(f_\ast\) for the entrywise application of \(f\), the
460	  nonsingular inverse is natural:
461	  \[
462	    \bigl(f_\ast A\bigr)^{-1} \;=\; f_\ast\bigl(A^{-1}\bigr).
463	  \]
464	\end{lemma}
465	\begin{proof}
466	
467	\leanok
468	  Since \(f_\ast A \cdot f_\ast(A^{-1}) = f_\ast(A A^{-1}) = f_\ast(1_{n\times n})
469	  = 1_{n\times n}\) (using \(A A^{-1} = 1\), \cref{lem:mathlib_mul_nonsing_inv}),
470	  the matrix \(f_\ast(A^{-1})\) is a right inverse of \(f_\ast A\), hence equals
471	  \((f_\ast A)^{-1}\). Proved directly in Lean.
472	\end{proof}
473	
474	\begin{lemma}
475	
476	\leanok
477	[Functoriality of entrywise application]
478	  \label{lem:gr_map_map_eq_of_comp}
479	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.map_map_eq_of_comp}
480	  Let \(f : R \to A\), \(g : A \to D\) and \(h : R \to D\) be ring
481	  homomorphisms with \(g \circ f = h\). Then for any matrix \(M\) over \(R\),
482	  applying \(f\) then \(g\) entrywise equals applying \(h\) entrywise:
483	  \(g_\ast(f_\ast M) = h_\ast M\).
484	\end{lemma}
485	\begin{proof}
486	
487	\leanok
488	  Immediate from functoriality of \(\operatorname{Mat}(-)\) (composition of
489	  entrywise maps). Proved directly in Lean.
490	\end{proof}
491	
492	\begin{lemma}
493	
494	\leanok
495	[Inverse cancellation in a composite]
496	  \label{lem:gr_inv_mul_inv_mul_cancel}
497	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.inv_mul_inv_mul_cancel}
498	  \uses{lem:mathlib_mul_nonsing_inv}
499	  Let \(A, B \in \operatorname{Mat}_{d\times d}(R)\) and
500	  \(M \in \operatorname{Mat}_{d\times e}(R)\) over a commutative ring, with
501	  \(\det A\) a unit. Then
502	  \[
503	    \bigl(B^{-1} A\bigr)\,\bigl(A^{-1} M\bigr) \;=\; B^{-1} M.
504	  \]
505	\end{lemma}
506	\begin{proof}
507	
508	\leanok
509	  By associativity and \(A A^{-1} = 1_{d\times d}\)
510	  (\cref{lem:mathlib_mul_nonsing_inv}). Proved directly in Lean.
511	\end{proof}

513	\begin{lemma}
514	
515	\leanok
516	[The \(I\)-minor of \(X^I\) is the identity]
517	  \label{lem:gr_universalMatrix_submatrix_self}
518	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.universalMatrix_submatrix_self}
519	  \uses{def:gr_universal_matrix}
520	  The \(I\)-minor of the universal matrix \(X^I\)
521	  (\cref{def:gr_universal_matrix}), with its \(I\)-columns read through the order
522	  isomorphism \(\{1,\dots,d\} \xrightarrow{\sim} I\), is the \(d\times d\)
523	  identity: \(X^I_I = 1_{d\times d}\). (This is the normalisation \(P^I_I = 1\)
524	  defining the chart.)
525	\end{lemma}
526	\begin{proof}
527	
528	\leanok
529	  By \cref{def:gr_universal_matrix} the \(q\)-column with \(q \in I\) is the
530	  standard basis vector \(e_p\) where \(p\) is the preimage of \(q\) under the
531	  order isomorphism \(\{1,\dots,d\} \xrightarrow{\sim} I\); so the \(I\)-minor
532	  is the identity. Proved directly in Lean.
533	\end{proof}
534	
535	\begin{lemma}
536	
537	\leanok
538	[The \(J\)-minor of the image matrix is the identity]
539	  \label{lem:gr_imageMatrix_submatrix_self}
540	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.imageMatrix_submatrix_self}
541	  \uses{def:gr_image_matrix, lem:gr_mul_submatrix_col, lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities}
542	  The \(J\)-minor of the image matrix \(M = (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\)
543	  (\cref{def:gr_image_matrix}) is the identity:
544	  \[
545	    M_J \;=\; (X^I_J)^{-1}\, X^I_J \;=\; 1_{d\times d}.
546	  \]
547	\end{lemma}
548	\begin{proof}
549	
550	\leanok
551	  The \(J\)-column submatrix of \(M = (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\) is
552	  \((X^I_J)^{-1}\) times the \(J\)-column submatrix of \(X^I\), i.e.
553	  \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I_J\), by \cref{lem:gr_mul_submatrix_col}; this is
554	  \(1_{d\times d}\) by the left-inverse identity
555	  (\cref{lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities}). Proved directly in Lean.
556	\end{proof}

560	\leanok

579	\begin{lemma}
580	
581	\leanok

585	  \uses{def:gr_universal_matrix, def:gr_image_matrix, def:gr_transition_pre, lem:gr_imageMatrix_submatrix_self}
586	  Applying the pre-localisation hom \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}\)
587	  (\cref{def:gr_transition_pre}) entrywise to the universal matrix \(X^J\) yields
588	  the image matrix:

590	    (\tilde\theta_{I,J})_\ast\, X^J \;=\; M \;=\; (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I .
591	  \]
592	\end{lemma}
593	\begin{proof}
594	
595	\leanok
596	  On a free entry \(x^J_{p,q}\) (\(q \notin J\)) this holds by the definition of
597	  \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}\) as evaluation at the entries of \(M\)
598	  (\cref{def:gr_transition_pre}). On a column \(q \in J\) the entry of \(X^J\) is
599	  an identity-block entry, which \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}\) carries to the

606	\leanok

608	  \label{lem:gr_transitionPreMap_minorDet}

610	  \uses{def:gr_transition_pre, def:gr_minor_det, def:gr_image_matrix, lem:gr_universalMatrix_map_transitionPreMap}
611	  For a third size-\(d\) subset \(K\), the pre-hom \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}\) sends the
612	  minor determinant \(P^J_K\) (\cref{def:gr_minor_det}) to the determinant of the
613	  \(K\)-minor of the image matrix \(M = (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\):
614	  \[
615	    \tilde\theta_{I,J}\bigl(P^J_K\bigr) \;=\; \det\bigl(M_K\bigr).
616	  \]
617	\end{lemma}
618	\begin{proof}
619	
620	\leanok
621	  The hom \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}\) commutes with determinants and with passing to
622	  a column submatrix, so \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}(P^J_K) =
623	  \det\bigl((\tilde\theta_{I,J})_\ast X^J\bigr)_K\); apply
624	  \cref{lem:gr_universalMatrix_map_transitionPreMap}. Proved directly in Lean.

627	\begin{lemma}
628	
629	\leanok
630	[Naturality of the image matrix]
631	  \label{lem:gr_imageMatrix_map_eq}
632	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.imageMatrix_map_eq}
633	  \uses{def:gr_image_matrix, lem:gr_map_map_eq_of_comp, def:gr_universalMinorInv, lem:gr_map_nonsing_inv, lem:gr_minorDet_unit, def:gr_universal_minor}
634	  Let \(X\) be a size-\(d\) subset and \(\iota : R^I_X \to D\) a ring
635	  homomorphism lying over the structure map \(R^I \to D\), i.e.\
636	  \(\iota \circ (R^I \to R^I_X) = (R^I \to D)\). Writing \(Y\) for the universal
637	  matrix \(X^I\) base-changed to \(D\) and \(Y_X\) for its \(X\)-minor, one has
638	  \[
639	    \iota_\ast\bigl((X^I_X)^{-1} X^I\bigr) \;=\; (Y_X)^{-1}\, Y .
640	  \]
641	\end{lemma}
642	\begin{proof}
643	
644	\leanok
645	  Distribute \(\iota_\ast\) across the product \(M = (X^I_X)^{-1} X^I\); the
646	  factor \((X^I_X)^{-1}\) passes through \(\iota_\ast\) as a Cramer inverse
647	  (\cref{lem:gr_map_nonsing_inv}, using \cref{lem:gr_minorDet_unit}), and both
648	  factors collapse to base changes over \(D\) by functoriality
649	  (\cref{lem:gr_map_map_eq_of_comp}) since \(\iota\) lies over \(R^I \to D\).
650	  Proved directly in Lean.
651	\end{proof}

653	\subsection*{Triple-overlap rings and the localised transition maps}
654	
655	The cocycle condition \(\theta_{I,K} = \theta_{I,J} \circ \theta_{J,K}\) cannot
656	be stated as a naive composition of the landed transition maps of
657	\cref{def:gr_transition}: the codomain of \(\theta_{J,K}\) (the ring
658	\(R^J[1/P^J_K]\)) differs from the domain of \(\theta_{I,J}\) (the ring
659	\(R^J[1/P^J_I]\)). The identity therefore lives over the \emph{triple-overlap}
660	rings obtained by inverting both relevant minors in each chart ring:
661	\[
662	  S_K := R^K[1/(P^K_I P^K_J)], \quad
663	  S_J := R^J[1/(P^J_I P^J_K)], \quad
664	  S_I := R^I[1/(P^I_J P^I_K)].
665	\]
666	
667	\begin{definition}
668	
669	\leanok
670	[Inclusion of an away-localisation into a double one (left)]
671	  \label{def:gr_away_incl_left}
672	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.awayInclLeft}
673	  \uses{lem:mathlib_away_lift, lem:mathlib_away_algebraMap_isUnit}
674	  For \(x, y \in R\), the canonical inclusion
675	  \(\iota^{\mathrm{L}}_{x,y} : R[1/x] \to R[1/(xy)]\) inverting the extra factor
676	  \(y\), defined as the away-localisation lift (\cref{lem:mathlib_away_lift}) of
677	  the structure map \(R \to R[1/(xy)]\) along \(x\) — well-defined because \(x\)
678	  is a unit of \(R[1/(xy)]\) (a factor of the unit \(xy\),
679	  \cref{lem:mathlib_away_algebraMap_isUnit}).
680	\end{definition}
681	
682	\begin{definition}
683	
684	\leanok
685	[Inclusion of an away-localisation into a double one (right)]
686	  \label{def:gr_away_incl_right}
687	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.awayInclRight}
688	  \uses{lem:mathlib_away_lift, lem:mathlib_away_algebraMap_isUnit}
689	  For \(x, y \in R\), the canonical inclusion
690	  \(\iota^{\mathrm{R}}_{x,y} : R[1/y] \to R[1/(xy)]\) inverting the extra factor
691	  \(x\), defined as the away-localisation lift (\cref{lem:mathlib_away_lift}) of
692	  \(R \to R[1/(xy)]\) along \(y\) — well-defined since \(y\) is a unit of
693	  \(R[1/(xy)]\) (\cref{lem:mathlib_away_algebraMap_isUnit}).
694	\end{definition}
695	
696	\begin{lemma}
697	
698	\leanok
699	[Left inclusion lies over \(R\)]
700	  \label{lem:gr_awayInclLeft_comp_algebraMap}
701	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.awayInclLeft_comp_algebraMap}
702	  \uses{def:gr_away_incl_left}
703	  The inclusion \(\iota^{\mathrm{L}}_{x,y}\) (\cref{def:gr_away_incl_left})
704	  intertwines the two structure maps:
705	  \(\iota^{\mathrm{L}}_{x,y} \circ (R \to R[1/x]) = (R \to R[1/(xy)])\).
706	\end{lemma}
707	\begin{proof}
708	
709	\leanok
710	  By the defining property of the away-localisation lift
711	  (\cref{lem:mathlib_away_lift}). Proved directly in Lean.
712	\end{proof}

765	
766	\begin{lemma}
767	
768	\leanok
769	[The cross minor becomes a unit after inclusion]
770	  \label{lem:gr_isUnit_incl_transitionPreMap_cross}
771	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.isUnit_incl_transitionPreMap_cross}
772	  \uses{lem:gr_transitionPreMap_minorDet, lem:gr_mul_submatrix_col, lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities, def:gr_image_matrix}
773	  Let \(A, B, C\) be size-\(d\) subsets and \(\iota : R^A_B \to D\) a ring
774	  homomorphism over the structure map \(R^A \to D\) under which the minor
775	  determinant \(P^A_C\) becomes a unit of \(D\). Then
776	  \(\iota\bigl(\tilde\theta_{A,B}(P^B_C)\bigr)\) is a unit of \(D\). Indeed
777	  \[
778	    \tilde\theta_{A,B}\bigl(P^B_C\bigr)
779	      = \det\bigl((X^A_B)^{-1} X^A_C\bigr)

849	\begin{lemma}
850	
851	\leanok
852	[Image-matrix identity over the triple overlap]
853	  \label{lem:gr_cocycle_imageMatrix_eq}
854	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.cocycle_imageMatrix_eq}
855	  \uses{lem:gr_imageMatrix_map_eq, def:gr_cocycle_theta_ij, def:gr_away_incl_right, def:gr_away_incl_left, lem:gr_awayInclRight_comp_algebraMap, lem:gr_awayInclLeft_comp_algebraMap, lem:gr_universalMatrix_map_transitionPreMap, lem:gr_map_map_eq_of_comp, lem:gr_map_nonsing_inv, lem:gr_mul_submatrix_col, lem:gr_inv_mul_inv_mul_cancel, lem:gr_isUnit_algebraMap_away_left, lem:gr_minorDet_unit, def:gr_image_matrix, def:gr_universal_minor}
856	  Over the triple-overlap ring \(S_I\), the image matrix of \(\theta_{I,K}\)
857	  equals \(\Theta_{I,J}\) applied entrywise to the image matrix of
858	  \(\theta_{J,K}\):
859	  \[
860	    \iota^{\mathrm{R}}_\ast\bigl((X^I_K)^{-1} X^I\bigr)
861	      \;=\;
862	      \bigl(\Theta_{I,J}\circ\iota^{\mathrm{R}}\bigr)_\ast
863	        \bigl((X^J_K)^{-1} X^J\bigr) .
864	  \]
865	  Both sides reduce to \((Y_K)^{-1} Y\), where \(Y\) is the universal matrix
866	  \(X^I\) base-changed to \(S_I\) and \(Y_K\) its \(K\)-minor.
867	\end{lemma}
868	\begin{proof}
869	
870	\leanok
871	  The left side is \((Y_K)^{-1} Y\) by naturality of the image matrix
872	  (\cref{lem:gr_imageMatrix_map_eq}, via \cref{lem:gr_awayInclRight_comp_algebraMap}).
873	  For the right side, \(\Theta_{I,J}\circ\iota^{\mathrm{R}}\) carries the chart
874	  ring \(R^J\) over \(\iota^{\mathrm{L}}\circ\tilde\theta_{I,J}\), so the image
875	  matrix of \(\theta_{J,K}\) maps to \((M^J_K)^{-1} M^J\) with
876	  \(M^J = (Y_J)^{-1} Y\) (\cref{lem:gr_universalMatrix_map_transitionPreMap},
877	  \cref{lem:gr_imageMatrix_map_eq}); the minor \(M^J_K = (Y_J)^{-1} Y_K\)
878	  (\cref{lem:gr_mul_submatrix_col}) inverts via \((AB)^{-1} = B^{-1}A^{-1}\), and
879	  the cancellation \((Y_K)^{-1} Y_J \cdot (Y_J)^{-1} Y = (Y_K)^{-1} Y\)
880	  (\cref{lem:gr_inv_mul_inv_mul_cancel}, using \(\det Y_J\) a unit by
881	  \cref{lem:gr_isUnit_algebraMap_away_left}) gives \((Y_K)^{-1} Y\). Proved
882	  directly in Lean.
883	\end{proof}

885	\begin{lemma}
886	
887	\leanok
888	[Cocycle condition for the transition maps]
889	  \label{lem:gr_cocycle}
890	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.cocycleCondition}
891	  \uses{def:gr_transition, def:gr_affine_chart, def:gr_image_matrix, lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities, lem:gr_transition_self, def:gr_cocycle_theta_ij, def:gr_cocycle_theta_jk, def:gr_cocycle_theta_ik, lem:gr_cocycle_imageMatrix_eq}
892	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Construction by gluing together
893	  % affine patches" (read from
894	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L838-L848).
895	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
896	  % "Note that $\theta_{I,I}$ is identity on $U^I_I = U^I$, and
897	  %  we leave it to the reader to verify that
898	  %  for any three subsets $I$, $J$ and $K$ of $\{ 1,\ldots, r\}$ of
899	  %  cardinality $d$, the co-cycle condition
900	  %  $\theta_{I,K}= \theta_{I,J}\theta_{J,K}$
901	  %  is satisfied."
902	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
903	  For any three subsets \(I, J, K \subseteq \{1, \dots, r\}\) of cardinality
904	  \(d\), the transition maps of \cref{def:gr_transition} satisfy the cocycle
905	  condition. As morphisms of schemes, on the triple overlap inside \(U^I\),
906	  \[
907	    \theta_{I,K} \;=\; \theta_{J,K} \circ \theta_{I,J},
908	  \]
909	  equivalently, as ring homomorphisms,
910	  \(\theta_{I,K} = \theta_{I,J} \circ \theta_{J,K}\) (the two read the same
911	  identity through the order-reversal of \(\Spec\)). Together with
912	  \(\theta_{I,I} = \id\), this is exactly the compatibility datum required to
913	  glue the charts.
914	\end{lemma}
915	
916	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Note that $\theta_{I,I}$ is identity on $U^I_I = U^I$, and
917	% we leave it to the reader to verify that
918	% for any three subsets $I$, $J$ and $K$ of $\{ 1,\ldots, r\}$ of
919	% cardinality $d$, the co-cycle condition
920	% $\theta_{I,K}= \theta_{I,J}\theta_{J,K}$
921	% is satisfied."  (references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex,
922	% L838-L848.)
923	\begin{proof}
924	  \uses{def:gr_transition, def:gr_affine_chart, def:gr_image_matrix, lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities, lem:gr_transition_self, def:gr_cocycle_theta_ij, def:gr_cocycle_theta_jk, def:gr_cocycle_theta_ik, lem:gr_cocycle_imageMatrix_eq}
925	  \leanok
926	  We verify the scheme-morphism form
927	  \(\theta_{I,K} = \theta_{J,K} \circ \theta_{I,J}\) by a direct matrix
928	  computation, valid over the open locus of \(U^I\) on which all the minors
929	  involved are invertible (namely \(P^I_J\) and \(P^I_K\), which suffices since
930	  the maps are determined by their restriction to a dense open and all rings are
931	  domains; the computation is an identity of matrices with entries in
932	  \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J, 1/P^I_K]\)).
933	
934	  Start from the matrix \(X^I\) (the universal point of \(U^I\)). By
935	  \cref{def:gr_transition},
936	  \[
937	    M^J \;:=\; \theta_{I,J}(X^I) \;=\; (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I,
938	  \]
939	  a \(d \times r\) matrix whose \(J\)-minor is the identity, hence a point of
940	  \(U^J\). Its \(K\)-minor is
941	  \[
942	    M^J_K \;=\; (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I_K,
943	    \qquad\text{so}\qquad
944	    (M^J_K)^{-1} \;=\; (X^I_K)^{-1} X^I_J,
945	  \]
946	  using \((AB)^{-1} = B^{-1} A^{-1}\) for the invertible \(d \times d\) matrices
947	  \(X^I_J\) and \(X^I_K\). Applying \(\theta_{J,K}\) to \(M^J\) gives
948	  \[
949	    \theta_{J,K}(M^J) \;=\; (M^J_K)^{-1} M^J
950	    \;=\; (X^I_K)^{-1} X^I_J \,\cdot\, (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I
951	    \;=\; (X^I_K)^{-1} X^I,
952	  \]
953	  the middle product \(X^I_J (X^I_J)^{-1} = 1_{d\times d}\) cancelling by
954	  associativity of matrix multiplication. The right-hand side is precisely
955	  \(\theta_{I,K}(X^I) = (X^I_K)^{-1} X^I\). Thus
956	  \(\theta_{J,K} \circ \theta_{I,J}\) and \(\theta_{I,K}\) agree on the
957	  universal matrix \(X^I\), hence agree as morphisms \(U^I \supseteq U^I_J \cap
958	  U^I_K \to U^K\). The special case \(K = I\) (resp.\ \(J = I\)) together with
959	  \(\theta_{I,I} = \id\) shows each \(\theta_{I,J}\) is an isomorphism onto its
960	  image with inverse \(\theta_{J,I}\), since
961	  \(\theta_{J,I} \circ \theta_{I,J} = \theta_{I,I} = \id\).
962	\end{proof}

964	\section{Scheme-level glue-data layer}
965	\label{sec:gr_glue_layer}
966	
967	The transition data of \cref{sec:gr_transition} lives at the level of rings. To
968	feed it to a scheme-gluing machine we must re-express each piece as a morphism in
969	the category of schemes: the charts \(U^I\), the principal-open overlaps
970	\(U^I_J\), their inclusions, and the transitions \(\theta_{I,J}\) all become
971	schemes and scheme morphisms by applying the contravariant functor \(\Spec\).
972	The lemmas of this section record those translations together with the two
973	genuinely geometric facts needed to assemble a glue datum: that each inclusion
974	\(U^I_J \hookrightarrow U^I\) is an open immersion, and that the fibre product of
975	two principal opens of \(\Spec A\) is again a principal open. They are
976	project-internal bridge results with no external source beyond the gluing
977	construction of \cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}.
978

916	\begin{lemma}\leanok
917	[L5b.2 — Nagata change of variables: monic in the distinguished variable]
918	  \label{lem:gf_nagata_monic_lastVar}
919	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.gf_nagata_monic_lastVar}
920	  \uses{def:gf_nagata_T, lem:gf_T_leadingcoeff_eq, lem:gf_finSuccEquiv_map_comm}
921	  % NOTE (variable convention): the project's univariate elimination uses
922	  %   MvPolynomial.finSuccEquiv, which singles out variable X_0 as the polynomial
923	  %   variable over the coefficient ring MvPolynomial (Fin n) A. The choice of which of
924	  %   the d = n+1 variables is "distinguished/eliminated" is immaterial (a renaming);
925	  %   the informal prose elsewhere says "last variable X_d" but the Lean uses X_0.
926	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, inductive step
927	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1766-L1768);
928	  % the Nagata substitution is the standard device realising the support-dimension drop.
929	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
930	  % "Therefore, the dimension of the support of $K\otimes_{A_g}T$
931	  %  as a $K\otimes_{A_g}(B_g)$-module is strictly less than $n$. Hence by
932	  %  induction on $n$ (applied to the data $A_g$, $B_g$, $T$),"
933	  % LEAN SIGNATURE (the scaffold creates the decl pinned above with this signature).
934	  %   A_g := Localization.Away g; map := MvPolynomial.map (algebraMap A A_g).
935	  %     (A : Type*) [CommRing A] [IsDomain A] (n : ℕ)
936	  %       (F : MvPolynomial (Fin (n + 1)) A) (hF : F ≠ 0) :
937	  %       ∃ (g : A) (_ : g ≠ 0)
938	  %         (e : MvPolynomial (Fin (n + 1)) A ≃ₐ[A] MvPolynomial (Fin (n + 1)) A),
939	  %         IsUnit
940	  %           (MvPolynomial.finSuccEquiv (Localization.Away g) n
941	  %             (MvPolynomial.map (algebraMap A (Localization.Away g)) (e F))).leadingCoeff
942	  %   The automorphism e is the triangular Nagata substitution; g is the (non-zero)
943	  %   leading coefficient that becomes a unit after inversion.
944	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (support-dimension drop).}
945	  Let \(A\) be a domain, \(n \ge 0\), and \(0 \ne F \in A[X_0, \dots, X_n]\). Then there
946	  exist a triangular \(A\)-algebra automorphism \(e\) of \(A[X_0, \dots, X_n]\) and a
947	  non-zero \(g \in A\) such that, after inverting \(g\), the image of \(e(F)\) in
948	  \(A_g[X_0, \dots, X_n]\), viewed as a univariate polynomial in \(X_0\) over
949	  \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n]\), has a \emph{unit} leading coefficient. Equivalently, \(e(F)\)
950	  is monic in \(X_0\) up to the unit \(g\) over \(A_g\).
951	\end{lemma}
952	
953	\begin{proof}
954	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1766-L1768).
955	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (support-dimension drop, Nagata normalisation).}
956	  Apply the Nagata substitution \(X_i \mapsto X_i + X_0^{r^{\,i}}\) for \(i = 1, \dots, n\)
957	  and \(X_0 \mapsto X_0\), with \(r\) larger than every exponent occurring in \(F\): it is a
958	  triangular (hence invertible) \(A\)-algebra automorphism \(e\) of \(A[X_0, \dots, X_n]\).
959	  Under this substitution the distinct monomials of \(F\) acquire distinct degrees in
960	  \(X_0\), so \(e(F)\), expanded as a polynomial in \(X_0\), has a unique top-degree term
961	  whose coefficient is the coefficient \(c \in A\) of the top total-degree monomial of
962	  \(F\); since \(F \ne 0\) this \(c \ne 0\). Put \(g := c\). The degree of \(e(F)\) in
963	  \(X_0\) equals the total degree of \(F\), and its leading coefficient in \(X_0\) is the
964	  constant \(g\). Passing to \(A_g = A[g^{-1}]\), the image of \(g\) is a unit, so the
965	  image of \(e(F)\) in \(A_g[X_0, \dots, X_n]\) is monic in \(X_0\) up to the unit \(g\).
966	\end{proof}

1058	  % "Therefore, the dimension of the support of $K\otimes_{A_g}T$
1059	  %  as a $K\otimes_{A_g}(B_g)$-module is strictly less than $n$. Hence by
1060	  %  induction on $n$ (applied to the data $A_g$, $B_g$, $T$),"
1061	  % LEAN SIGNATURE (the scaffold creates the decl pinned above with this signature).
1062	  %   Write A_g := Localization.Away g and T_g := LocalizedModule (Submonoid.powers g) T.
1063	  %     (A : Type*) [CommRing A] [IsDomain A] [IsNoetherianRing A]
1064	  %       (d : ℕ) (hd : 0 < d) (T : Type*) [AddCommGroup T]
1065	  %       [Module (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) T]
1066	  %       [Module.Finite (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) T]
1067	  %       [Module A T] [IsScalarTower A (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) T]
1068	  %       (htors : Module.IsTorsion (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) T) :
1069	  %       ∃ (g : A) (_ : g ≠ 0) (m' : ℕ) (_ : m' < d)
1070	  %         (_ : Module (MvPolynomial (Fin m') (Localization.Away g))
1071	  %                 (LocalizedModule (Submonoid.powers g) T))
1072	  %         -- NOTE: the redundant canonical `Module A_g T_g` existential was
1073	  %         -- dropped from the landed decl (it is always `inferInstance`; bundling it created an
1074	  %         -- opaque fvar breaking OreLocalization instance matching at the L5 use-site).
1075	  %         (_ : IsScalarTower (Localization.Away g)
1076	  %                 (MvPolynomial (Fin m') (Localization.Away g))
1077	  %                 (LocalizedModule (Submonoid.powers g) T)),
1078	  %         Module.Finite (MvPolynomial (Fin m') (Localization.Away g))
1079	  %           (LocalizedModule (Submonoid.powers g) T)
1080	  %   one may always take m' = d - 1 (single-variable elimination); the strong induction
1081	  %   accepts any m' < d.
1082	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (support-dimension drop).}
1083	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain, \(d \ge 1\), and \(T\) a finite \emph{torsion} module
1084	  over \(P_d := A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\) (with its induced \(A\)-module structure). Then there
1085	  exist a non-zero \(g \in A\) and an integer \(m' < d\) such that, after inverting \(g\),
1086	  the localised module \(T_g := T[g^{-1}]\) is module-finite over the polynomial ring
1087	  \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}]\) in \(m'\) variables over the base ring \(A_g = A[g^{-1}]\);
1088	  that is, \(T_g\) carries compatible \(A_g\)- and \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}]\)-module
1089	  structures (via the scalar tower \(A_g \to A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}]\)) and is finite over
1090	  \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}]\). One may always take \(m' = d - 1\).
1091	\end{lemma}
1092	
1093	\begin{proof}
1094	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1766-L1768).
1095	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (support-dimension drop).}
1096	  \uses{lem:gf_torsion_annihilator, lem:gf_nagata_monic_lastVar,
1097	        lem:gf_mvPolynomial_quotient_finite_monic, lem:gf_pullback_module_transport,
1098	        lem:gf_finite_of_quotient_ringequiv, lem:gf_islocalizedmodule_restrictscalars}
1099	  Write \(d = (d-1) + 1\) and \(P_d = A[X_0, \dots, X_{d-1}]\) (the distinguished variable
1100	  \(X_0\) is the one eliminated in the single-variable step; cf.\ \cref{lem:gf_nagata_monic_lastVar}).
1101	
1102	  \emph{Annihilator.} By \cref{lem:gf_torsion_annihilator} there is a non-zero
1103	  \(F \in \operatorname{Ann}_{P_d}(T)\), and \(T\) is a finite module over \(P_d/(F)\).
1104	
1105	  \emph{Nagata normalisation.} Applying \cref{lem:gf_nagata_monic_lastVar} to \(F \ne 0\)
1106	  yields a triangular \(A\)-algebra automorphism \(e\) of \(P_d\) and a non-zero
1107	  \(g \in A\) such that the image of \(e(F)\) in \(A_g[X_0, \dots, X_{d-1}]\) has a unit
1108	  leading coefficient in \(X_0\). The automorphism \(e\) induces a ring isomorphism
1109	  \(P_d/(F) \cong P_d/(e\,F)\), so \(T\) is also finite over \(P_d/(e\,F)\). Localising at
1110	  the powers of \(g\) (localisation commutes with the quotient), \(T_g\) is finite over
1111	  \(P_d[g^{-1}]/(e\,F) = A_g[X_0, \dots, X_{d-1}]/(e\,F)\).
1112	
1113	  \emph{Elimination.} Apply the shared engine
1114	  \cref{lem:gf_mvPolynomial_quotient_finite_monic} with \(R = A_g\) and \(p\) the image of
1115	  \(e(F)\) (whose leading coefficient in \(X_0\) is a unit): the quotient
1116	  \(A_g[X_0, \dots, X_{d-1}]/(e\,F)\) is module-finite over the coefficient subring
1117	  \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{d-1}]\), a polynomial ring in \(m' := d - 1\) variables over
1118	  \(A_g\).
1119	
1120	  \emph{Localisation transport.} The elimination step records module-finiteness of the
1121	  \emph{quotient ring} over its coefficient subring, but the conclusion of the lemma is
1122	  about a localisation of the torsion module \(T\). Making the two agree is the
1123	  mathematical content of this step, which proceeds as follows.
1124	
1125	  Two distinct localisations of \(T\) are in play and must be identified. The natural
1126	  setting for the elimination is the localisation \(T'_g := T[\,\mathcal{C}^{-1}]\) of \(T\)
1127	  at the multiplicative set \(\mathcal{C} = \{\,c \cdot g^{\,k} : k \ge 0\,\}\) of constant
1128	  polynomials \(g^k\) inside \(P_d\); this localisation carries, for free, the full
1129	  module structure over the localised polynomial ring
1130	  \(P_d[g^{-1}] = A_g[X_0, \dots, X_{d-1}]\), its finiteness, and the quotient structure by
1131	  \((e\,F)\). The \emph{goal}, however, is phrased with \(T_g := T[g^{-1}]\), the
1132	  localisation of \(T\) at the powers of the single element \(g \in A\). One first proves
1133	  \(T'_g\) module-finite over \(R := A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}]\) and then transports that
1134	  finiteness to \(T_g\).
1135	
1136	  The change of variables \(e\) is transported to the localised polynomial ring as a
1137	  \emph{ring isomorphism} \(\bar e : P_d[g^{-1}] \xrightarrow{\sim} P_d[g^{-1}]\), obtained
1138	  by localising \(e\) at \(\mathcal{C}\) (which \(e\) preserves, since \(e\) fixes the
1139	  constants \(c\,X^0\)); it carries the localised annihilator \(F_g\) to the
1140	  Nagata-normalised generator \(G\) and fixes the constants coming from the base. Passing
1141	  to quotients, \(\bar e\) descends to a ring isomorphism
1142	  \(\psi : P_d[g^{-1}]/(F_g) \xrightarrow{\sim} P_d[g^{-1}]/(G)\) of the two quotient rings
1143	  (using that the image of the ideal \((F_g)\) under \(\bar e\) is \((G)\)). Both quotient
1144	  rings act on \(T'_g\), and module-finiteness over \(R\) of the elimination-side quotient
1145	  \(P_d[g^{-1}]/(G)\) is transported across \(\psi\) — a ring isomorphism of the
1146	  \emph{acting} ring compatible with the fixed \(R\)-algebra structure — by
1147	  \cref{lem:gf_finite_of_quotient_ringequiv}, yielding \(T'_g\) module-finite over \(R\).
1148	
1149	  Finally the two localisations are identified. Restricting scalars of the canonical
1150	  \(P_d\)-localisation map of \(T\) along \(A \to P_d\) exhibits \(T'_g\) as a localisation
1151	  of \(T\) \emph{at the powers of \(g\) over \(A\)} — this descent of the
1152	  localised-module property along the scalar tower is
1153	  \cref{lem:gf_islocalizedmodule_restrictscalars} — so the universal property produces a
1154	  linear isomorphism \(T_g \cong T'_g\) identifying the two.
1155	
1156	  \emph{Action-diamond subtlety.} The conclusion of the lemma requires an
1157	  \(A_g\)-module structure on \(T_g\) sitting in a scalar tower
1158	  \(A_g \to A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}] \to T_g\). One cannot simply install an anonymous
1159	  \(A_g\)-action by fiat: \(T_g = T[g^{-1}]\) already carries its \emph{canonical}
1160	  localisation action of \(A_g\), and this canonical action is the one the scalar-tower
1161	  requirement sees. Hence the identification \(T_g \cong T'_g\) must be promoted to an
1162	  \(A_g\)-linear isomorphism — which it is, because the transported action agrees with the
1163	  canonical localisation action on the \(A_g\)-constants: the constant
1164	  \(\mathcal{C}\)-action of \(A_g\) on \(T'_g\) factors through the original \(A\)-action on
1165	  \(T\) exactly as the canonical \(g\)-localisation action does. With the isomorphism made
1166	  \(A_g\)-linear, the \(R\)-module structure and finiteness pull back from \(T'_g\) to
1167	  \(T_g\) by \cref{lem:gf_pullback_module_transport}, and the scalar tower
1168	  \(A_g \to R \to T_g\) follows.
1169	
1170	  \emph{Transitivity.} Composing the two module-finite extensions, \(T_g\) is finite over
1171	  \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}]\). Since \(m' = d - 1 < d\) (using \(d \ge 1\)), this is the
1172	  required reindexing; no Krull-dimension theory is needed, the number of variables
1173	  dropping by exactly one through the single-variable elimination.
1174	\end{proof}

1176	\subsubsection{Module-structure transport helpers (reindex)}
1177	\label{sec:gf_reindex_transport_helpers}
1178	
1179	The localisation-transport step of \cref{lem:gf_torsion_reindex} moves a module structure
1180	and its finiteness across (i) an additive equivalence of the underlying groups, (ii) a ring
1181	isomorphism of the acting ring, and (iii) a change of localising monoid along a scalar
1182	tower. The following helper lemmas package those three transport principles. They are general
1183	module-theory facts; no external source is cited.
1184	
1185	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1186	[Module transport along an additive equivalence]
1187	  \label{lem:gf_pullback_module_transport}
1188	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.pullbackModuleAddEquiv}
1189	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.finite_of_pullbackModuleAddEquiv}
1190	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.pullback_isScalarTower}
1191	  Let \(e : M \xrightarrow{\sim} N\) be an additive equivalence and let \(M\) carry an
1192	  \(R\)-module structure. Pulling the action back along \(e\) by
1193	  \(r \cdot y := e\big(r \cdot e^{-1}(y)\big)\) makes \(N\) an \(R\)-module for which \(e\)
1194	  is \(R\)-linear. Under this transported structure: (a) if \(M\) is module-finite over
1195	  \(R\) then so is \(N\); and (b) if \(M\) additionally carries a compatible action of a
1196	  base ring \(A_g\) with scalar tower \(A_g \to R \to M\), then the pulled-back actions on
1197	  \(N\) again form a scalar tower \(A_g \to R \to N\).
1198	\end{lemma}
1199	\begin{proof}
1200	  The module axioms for \(y \mapsto e(r \cdot e^{-1}(y))\) follow termwise from those on
1201	  \(M\) together with the fact that \(e\) and \(e^{-1}\) are additive and mutually inverse;
1202	  by construction \(e\) is then an \(R\)-linear isomorphism. Statement (a) follows by
1203	  transporting module-finiteness across the \(R\)-linear isomorphism \(e\).
1204	  Statement (b) checks the scalar-tower identity
1205	  \((a\,r)\cdot y = a\cdot(r\cdot y)\) on \(N\) by transporting it across \(e\) and applying
1206	  associativity of the scalar action on \(M\).
1207	\end{proof}
1208	
1209	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1210	[Finite transport along a ring isomorphism of the acting ring]
1211	  \label{lem:gf_finite_of_quotient_ringequiv}
1212	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.finite_of_quotientRingEquiv}
1213	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring and let \(\psi : B_1 \xrightarrow{\sim} B_2\) be a ring
1214	  isomorphism between two \(R\)-algebras that is compatible with the structure maps, i.e.\
1215	  \(\psi \circ (\text{algebra map } R \to B_1) = (\text{algebra map } R \to B_2)\). If
1216	  \(B_2\) is module-finite over \(R\) and \(M\) is module-finite over \(B_1\) (with the
1217	  scalar tower \(R \to B_1 \to M\)), then \(M\) is module-finite over \(R\).
1218	\end{lemma}
1219	\begin{proof}
1220	  Compatibility of \(\psi^{-1}\) with the structure maps follows from that of \(\psi\), so
1221	  \(\psi^{-1}\) is an \(R\)-algebra isomorphism; transporting \(R\)-finiteness of \(B_2\)
1222	  across it makes \(B_1\) module-finite over \(R\). Transitivity of module-finiteness through
1223	  the scalar tower \(R \to B_1 \to M\) then gives \(M\) module-finite over \(R\).
1224	\end{proof}
1225	
1226	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1227	[Descent of a localised-module structure along a scalar tower]
1228	  \label{lem:gf_islocalizedmodule_restrictscalars}
1229	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.isLocalizedModule_restrictScalars}
1230	  Let \(A_g \to R\) be a ring map with compatible \(A_g\)- and \(R\)-module structures on
1231	  \(M\) and \(M'\) (scalar towers \(A_g \to R \to M\) and \(A_g \to R \to M'\)), and let
1232	  \(S \subseteq A_g\) be a multiplicative subset. If an \(R\)-linear map
1233	  \(f : M \to M'\) realises \(M'\) as the localisation of \(M\) at the image submonoid
1234	  \(S \cdot 1 \subseteq R\) over \(R\), then its restriction of scalars to \(A_g\) realises
1235	  \(M'\) as the localisation of \(M\) at \(S\) over \(A_g\).
1236	\end{lemma}
1237	\begin{proof}
1238	  One verifies the universal property of a localised module for the restricted map. Each
1239	  \(s \in S\) acts invertibly on \(M'\): its action coincides, via the scalar tower, with
1240	  that of its image in \(R\), which is a unit because \(f\) is a localisation at the image
1241	  submonoid. Surjectivity onto \(M'\) by fractions \(x/s\) with \(s \in S\) and the
1242	  equality criterion likewise descend from the corresponding image-submonoid statements,
1243	  rewriting each \(R\)-scalar by an \(A_g\)-scalar through the scalar tower. Assembling
1244	  these conditions gives the claim.
1245	\end{proof}

1247	\begin{lemma}[Localisation identification across an associated denominator]
1248	  \label{lem:isLocalization_away_mul_of_associated}
1249	  \lean{IsLocalization.Away.mul_of_associated}
1250	  \mathlibok
1251	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
1252	  Let \(R \to S \to T\) be a tower of commutative rings with compatible structure maps, let
1253	  \(x, z \in R\) and \(y \in S\), and suppose \(S\) is the localisation of \(R\) away from
1254	  \(x\) and \(T\) is the localisation of \(S\) away from \(y\). If the image of \(z\) in \(S\)
1255	  is associated to \(y\) (the two differ by a unit), then \(T\) is the localisation of \(R\)
1256	  away from the single product \(x z\); that is, \(T \cong R[(x z)^{-1}]\).
1257	\end{lemma}

1259	\begin{lemma}[Freeness transports across a semilinear ring isomorphism]
1260	  \label{lem:module_free_of_ringEquiv}
1261	  \lean{Module.Free.of_ringEquiv}
1262	  \mathlibok
1263	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
1264	  Let \(R, R'\) be rings, \(M\) an \(R\)-module and \(M'\) an \(R'\)-module, and let
1265	  \(e : M \xrightarrow{\sim} M'\) be an additive isomorphism that is semilinear over a ring
1266	  isomorphism \(\sigma : R \xrightarrow{\sim} R'\), i.e.\ \(e(r \cdot m) = \sigma(r) \cdot e(m)\).
1267	  If \(M\) is free over \(R\), then \(M'\) is free over \(R'\).
1268	\end{lemma}

1270	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1271	[L5 step-4 helper — descent of freeness across a tower of \(\mathrm{Away}\) localisations]
1272	  \label{lem:gf_away_tower_descent}
1273	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.free_localizationAway_of_away_tower}
1274	  \uses{lem:isLocalization_away_mul_of_associated, lem:module_free_of_ringEquiv}
1275	  % LEAN DEPS: the localisation-transport primitives the Lean proof consumes are
1276	  %   IsLocalization.surj                          (clear the denominator of h over A_g)
1277	  %   IsLocalization.map_units                     (the cleared denominator s ∈ powers g is a unit)
1278	  %   IsLocalization.Away.mul_of_associated        (key ring identification: Away(g·a) on A_h)
1279	  %   IsLocalization.algEquiv                      (ring iso Away(g·a) ≅ A_h)
1280	  %   Module.Free.of_ringEquiv                     (transport freeness across that ring iso)
1281	  %   IsLocalizedModule.linearEquiv                (the σ-semilinear module equivalence)
1282	  %   LinearEquiv.extendScalarsOfIsLocalization    (upgrade the R-linear equiv to Away(g·a)-linear)
1283	  % The one primitive NOT packaged by Mathlib — that the composite localisation map
1284	  %   ψ := mkLinearMap(powers h) ∘ mkLinearMap(powers g) : T → (T_g)_h
1285	  % satisfies IsLocalizedModule (powers (g·a)) ψ — is proved directly from the three
1286	  % IsLocalizedModule axioms (units act invertibly, surjectivity up to denominators, the
1287	  % equality criterion); there is no packaged localisation-of-localisation lemma to invoke.
1288	  % NOTE: the closed Lean proof DID find a packaged route — the composite-localisation
1289	  % `IsLocalizedModule (powers (g·a)) ψ` step goes through `IsBaseChange.comp` via
1290	  % `isLocalizedModule_iff_isBaseChange`, so the "no packaged lemma" remark above is superseded.
1291	  % WITNESS: the conclusion holds at the SINGLE product f := g·a (not at a square f²).
1292	  Let \(A\) be a domain and \(T\) an \(A\)-module. Let \(g \in A\), \(g \ne 0\), and let
1293	  \(h \in A_g := \mathrm{Away}(g)\) be non-zero. Write
1294	  \((T_g)_h\) for the iterated localisation of \(T\) — first away from \(g\), then away from
1295	  \(h\) — and \((A_g)_h := \mathrm{Away}(h)\) for the corresponding iterated localisation of the
1296	  base ring. If \((T_g)_h\) is free over \((A_g)_h\), then there exists \(f \in A\),
1297	  \(f \ne 0\), such that the single localisation \(T_f\) is free over \(A_f := \mathrm{Away}(f)\).
1298	  Concretely the witness is the single product \(f := g\,a\) for an element \(a \in A\) whose
1299	  image in \(A_g\) is associated to \(h\).
1300	\end{lemma}
1301	
1302	\begin{proof}
1303	  \uses{lem:isLocalization_away_mul_of_associated, lem:module_free_of_ringEquiv}
1304	  \emph{Clear the denominator of \(h\).} As \(h\) lies in \(A_g = \mathrm{Away}(g)\),
1305	  surjectivity of the localisation map \(A \to A_g\) onto fractions yields \(a \in A\) and a
1306	  power \(s\) of \(g\) with \(h \cdot \bar s = \bar a\) in \(A_g\), where \(\bar{\,\cdot\,}\)
1307	  denotes the image in \(A_g\). The image \(\bar s\) is a unit (a power of \(g\) is invertible
1308	  in \(A_g\)), so \(\bar a = h\,\bar s\) is associated to \(h\); in particular \(\bar a \ne 0\)
1309	  (because \(h \ne 0\)), hence \(a \ne 0\). Since \(A\) is a domain and \(g, a \ne 0\), the
1310	  product \(f := g\,a\) is non-zero.
1311	
1312	  \emph{Identify the base rings.} The associatedness of \(\bar a\) and \(h\), together with the
1313	  tower \(A \to A_g \to A_h\), identifies the iterated localisation
1314	  \((A_g)_h = \mathrm{Away}(h)\) with the single localisation of \(A\) away from the product
1315	  \(g\,a\): by \cref{lem:isLocalization_away_mul_of_associated} (with \(x = g\), \(z = a\),
1316	  \(y = h\)) the ring \(A_h\) is a localisation of \(A\) away from \(g\,a\), and uniqueness of
1317	  localisation furnishes a ring isomorphism \(\mathrm{Away}(g\,a) \xrightarrow{\sim} A_h\).
1318	
1319	  \emph{Identify the modules.} The composite localisation map
1320	  \[
1321	    \psi : T \longrightarrow (T_g)_h, \qquad
1322	    \psi := \mathrm{mk}_{\langle h\rangle} \circ \mathrm{mk}_{\langle g\rangle},
1323	  \]
1324	  first into \(T_g\) and then into \((T_g)_h\), realises \((T_g)_h\) as the localisation of
1325	  \(T\) at the powers of \(g\,a\): every \(g^n a^m\) acts invertibly on \((T_g)_h\), \(\psi\) is
1326	  surjective up to such denominators, and its kernel is the expected one. This
1327	  localisation-of-localisation identity is verified directly from the defining properties of a
1328	  localised module; no packaged lemma supplies it.
1329	
1330	  \emph{Transport freeness.} By hypothesis \((T_g)_h\) is free over \(A_h\). The ring
1331	  isomorphism \(\mathrm{Away}(g\,a) \cong A_h\) and the induced semilinear module equivalence
1332	  \(T_{g\,a} \simeq (T_g)_h\) — assembled from the localised-module identification of the
1333	  previous step and promoted to an \(\mathrm{Away}(g\,a)\)-linear equivalence — carry this
1334	  freeness back by \cref{lem:module_free_of_ringEquiv}: \(T_{g\,a}\) is free over
1335	  \(\mathrm{Away}(g\,a)\). Taking \(f := g\,a\) gives \(T_f\) free over \(A_f\), as required.
1336	\end{proof}

1338	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1339	[L5 — polynomial-ring core of generic freeness]
1340	  \label{lem:gf_polynomial_core}

1349	        lem:gf_splice_shortExact_free_transport, lem:gf_generic_rank_ses,
1350	        lem:gf_torsion_reindex, lem:gf_away_tower_descent}

1356	  %  $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$-modules of the form
1357	  %  $0\to A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]^{\oplus m} \to B_g \to T \to 0$ where $T$ is a finite
1358	  %  torsion module over $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$. Therefore, the dimension of the
1359	  %  support of $K\otimes_{A_g}T$ as a $K\otimes_{A_g}(B_g)$-module is strictly less
1360	  %  than $n$. Hence by induction on $n$ (applied to the data $A_g$, $B_g$, $T$),
1361	  %  there exists some $h\ne 0$ in $A$ with $T_h$ free over $A_{gh}$. Taking $f=gh$,
1362	  %  the lemma follows from the above short exact sequence."
1363	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (polynomial-ring
1364	  core of the induction).}
1365	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain and \(d \ge 0\). Let \(N\) be a finite module over
1366	  the polynomial ring \(A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\), regarded as an \(A\)-module. Then there
1367	  exists \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), such that \(N_f\) is free over \(A_f\).
1368	
1369	\end{lemma}
1370	
1371	\begin{proof}
1372	  \uses{lem:gf_finite_module, lem:gf_splice_shortExact, lem:gf_torsion_base,
1373	        lem:gf_splice_shortExact_free_transport, lem:gf_generic_rank_ses,
1374	        lem:gf_torsion_reindex, lem:gf_away_tower_descent}
1375	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1760-L1772).
1376	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (polynomial-ring core).}
1377	  % LEAN PROOF STRUCTURE: the induction must generalise the base domain A
1378	  % TOGETHER WITH N. Formally, prove
1379	  %   ∀ d, ∀ (A) [CommRing A] [IsDomain A] [IsNoetherianRing A] (N) [..module data..],
1380	  %        ∃ f : A, f ≠ 0 ∧ Module.Free (Localization.Away f)
1381	  %                              (LocalizedModule (Submonoid.powers f) N)
1382	  % by Nat.strong_induction_on d, with A (and its CommRing/IsDomain/IsNoetherianRing
1383	  % instances) AND all of N's d-dependent module instances REVERTED INTO THE MOTIVE.
1384	  % This is load-bearing, and is the real cause of the prior stall: the reindex
1385	  % lem:gf_torsion_reindex changes the base ring from A to A_g = Localization.Away g, so
1386	  % the inductive hypothesis must be applied at base A_g, NOT A. An IH that fixes A would
1387	  % not typecheck on the reindexed module T_g (which is finite over
1388	  % MvPolynomial (Fin m') A_g). A_g remains a noetherian domain (a localisation of a
1389	  % noetherian domain), so the motive's hypotheses hold at A_g. Because `A` is already an
1390	  % explicit argument of the pinned signature, this is a reversion of A into the motive,
1391	  % NOT a change to the public signature. See analogies/gf-generic-rank-ses.md.
1392	  We argue by strong induction on \(d\), \emph{generalising the base domain \(A\)} (along
1393	  with the module \(N\)): the inductive hypothesis is available for every \(d' < d\),
1394	  every noetherian domain in place of \(A\), and every finite module over the
1395	  corresponding polynomial ring.
1396	
1397	  \emph{Base case \(d = 0\).} The ring \(P_0 = A[X_1, \dots, X_0]\) is \(A\) itself, so
1398	  \(N\) is a finite \(A\)-module and the claim is exactly the finite-module leaf
1399	  \cref{lem:gf_finite_module}.
1400	
1401	  \emph{Inductive step \(d \ge 1\).} If \(N\) is already a torsion \(P_d\)-module over
1402	  \(A\) — equivalently \(N_K = 0\) for \(K = \mathrm{Frac}(A)\) — then the torsion base
1403	  case \cref{lem:gf_torsion_base} produces \(f \ne 0\) with \(N_f = 0\), which is free
1404	  over \(A_f\). Otherwise we proceed in five steps.
1405	  \begin{enumerate}
1406	    \item \textbf{Generic-rank SES.} Apply \cref{lem:gf_generic_rank_ses} to \(N\) over
1407	      \(P_d = A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\): there are \(m \in \mathbb{N}\) and an injective
1408	      \(P_d\)-linear map \(\varphi : P_d^{\oplus m} \to N\) with torsion cokernel
1409	      \(T := N / \operatorname{im}\varphi\), i.e.\ a short exact sequence of
1410	      \(P_d\)-modules
1411	      \[
1412	        0 \to P_d^{\oplus m} \xrightarrow{\ \varphi\ } N \to T \to 0,
1413	        \qquad T \text{ torsion}.
1414	      \]
1415	      No element of \(A\) is inverted at this step.
1416	    \item \textbf{Reindex the torsion cokernel.} Apply \cref{lem:gf_torsion_reindex} to the
1417	      finite torsion module \(T\): there are \(g \ne 0\) in \(A\) and \(m' < d\) such that
1418	      the localisation \(T_g\) is module-finite over the polynomial ring
1419	      \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}]\) in fewer variables over the base ring \(A_g = A[g^{-1}]\).
1420	    \item \textbf{Inductive hypothesis at the new base \(A_g\).} Since \(A_g\) is again a
1421	      noetherian domain and \(m' < d\), the inductive hypothesis applies \emph{at base
1422	      \(A_g\)} to the finite \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}]\)-module \(T_g\): there is
1423	      \(h \ne 0\) in \(A_g\) with \((T_g)_h\) free over \((A_g)_h\). This is exactly the
1424	      point at which the base ring of the induction must be allowed to change from \(A\)
1425	      to \(A_g\).
1426	    \item \textbf{Descend the witness from \(A_g\) to \(A\).} Clearing the denominator of
1427	      \(h\) over \(A_g\) produces a numerator \(a \in A\) whose image in \(A_g\) is associated
1428	      to \(h\); then the iterated localisation \((A_g)_h\) is the single localisation
1429	      \(A_{g a}\) and \((T_g)_h\) is \(T_{g a}\). Thus freeness of \((T_g)_h\) over
1430	      \((A_g)_h\) transports to freeness of \(T_{g a}\) over \(A_{g a}\) by the
1431	      iterated-\(\mathrm{Away}\)-tower descent \cref{lem:gf_away_tower_descent}. Set
1432	      \(f := g a \ne 0\) — the single product, \emph{not} a square — so that \(T_f\) is free
1433	      over \(A_f\).
1434	      % NOTE: the tower-descent helper lem:gf_away_tower_descent
1435	      % (free_localizationAway_of_away_tower) is now CLOSED and axiom-clean. The L5 sorry that
1436	      % remains is NOT a missing math step — it is a Lean elaboration blocker: the
1437	      % `OreLocalization` instance *presentations* on the localised quotient `(N ⧸ range φ)_g`
1438	      % (CommSemiring / AddCommMonoid / Module-SMul) emitted by `gf_torsion_reindex` are defeq
1439	      % but not instance-transparent-equal to the presentations the helper input expects. The
1440	      % clean fix is non-local: make `gf_torsion_reindex` emit its conclusion over the canonical
1441	      % `OreLocalization.*` instances (its final `exact` / existential binders), after which the
1442	      % recorded five-line assembly closes verbatim. Do NOT re-dispatch a raw L5 prover round
1443	      % without that instance-alignment refactor first.
1444	    \item \textbf{Splice.} The left end \(P_d^{\oplus m}\) of the sequence is a free
1445	      \(A\)-module (the polynomial ring \(P_d = A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\) is \(A\)-free, hence
1446	      so is any finite direct power; note \(P_d^{\oplus m}\) is \emph{not} module-finite
1447	      over \(A\), but freeness is all the splice requires), and a free module stays free
1448	      under the localisation \(A \to A_f\); so \(P_d^{\oplus m}\) localises to a free
1449	      \(A_f\)-module. With both ends free over \(A_f\) after inverting \(f = g a\),
1450	      \cref{lem:gf_splice_shortExact} splices the localised short exact sequence to
1451	      conclude that the middle term \(N_f\) is free over \(A_f\). Taking \(f = g a\)
1452	      completes the induction.
1453	  \end{enumerate}
1454	\end{proof}
1455	
1456	\subsection{Supporting finite-module helpers}
1457	\label{sec:gf_finite_helpers}
1458	
1459	The following two lemmas package the finite \(A\)-module leaf in the two forms
1460	(flatness and module-finiteness) used by the dévissage chain.
1461	
1462	\begin{lemma}
1463	
1464	
1465	
1466	\leanok
1467	[Flat localization away, finite module]
1468	  \label{lem:gf_flat_finite}
1469	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.exists_flat_localizationAway_of_finite}
1470	  \uses{lem:gf_finite_module}
1471	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain and \(M\) a finite \(A\)-module. Then there exists
1472	  \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), such that \(M_f\) is flat over \(A_f\).
1473	\end{lemma}
1474	\begin{proof}
1475	  \uses{lem:gf_finite_module}
1476	  By \cref{lem:gf_finite_module} \(M_f\) is free over \(A_f\); free modules are flat.
1477	\end{proof}
1478	
1479	\begin{lemma}
1480	
1481	
1482	
1483	\leanok
1484	[Free localization away, module-finite variant]
1485	  \label{lem:gf_free_moduleFinite}
1486	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.exists_free_localizationAway_of_moduleFinite}
1487	  \uses{lem:gf_finite_module}
1488	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain, \(B\) a module-finite \(A\)-algebra, and \(M\) a
1489	  finite \(B\)-module with the compatible \(A\)-module structure via the scalar tower
1490	  \(A \to B \to M\). Then there exists \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), such that \(M_f\)
1491	  is free over \(A_f\).
1492	\end{lemma}
1493	\begin{proof}
1494	  \uses{lem:gf_finite_module}
1495	  Since \(B\) is module-finite over \(A\) and \(M\) is module-finite over \(B\),
1496	  transitivity of module-finiteness makes \(M\) a finite \(A\)-module.
1497	  \cref{lem:gf_finite_module} then gives the required \(f\).
1498	\end{proof}

1500	\subsection{Geometric bridges for the affine assembly}
1501	\label{sec:gf_geometric_bridges}
1502	
1503	The geometric form of generic flatness is assembled from the algebraic core by
1504	restricting to a non-empty affine open of \(S\) and working patchwise over a finite
1505	affine cover of the preimage. Two genuinely geometric steps are isolated here as
1506	named bridges: the affine-local finiteness of the section module of a finite-type
1507	quasi-coherent sheaf (G1), feeding the algebraic core its finite-module input; and
1508	the flat-locality assembly (G3), promoting per-patch freeness to flatness of the
1509	restricted sheaf over the base open. Neither is supplied directly by Mathlib: Mathlib
1510	does not provide the affine-local identification of \(\F|_W\) with the associated sheaf
1511	of \(\Gamma(\F, W)\) together with the finiteness this transports.
1512	
1513	% ============================================================================
1514	% G1 decomposition (Stacks 01PB). The affine-local finiteness of the section
1515	% module splits into TWO halves:
1516	%   Half 1 — LOCALITY reduction (already proved in Lean): if the section module
1517	%     is finite on each member of a finite basic-open cover, it is finite on the
1518	%     affine. Blocks: lem:finite_localizedModule_transfer,
1519	%     lem:gf_finite_sections_of_basicOpen_finite_cover.
1520	%   Half 2 — finite-type BASE CASE: on a globally-generated affine the section
1521	%     module is finite, via Gamma-exactness on affine quasi-coherent modules.
1522	%     Blocks (seams): lem:gf_finiteType_affine_finite_cover_generated (seam 1),
1523	%     lem:gf_affine_qcoh_Gamma_epi (seam 2),
1524	%     lem:gf_qcoh_finite_sections_globally_generated (base case = seam 2 + 3).
1525	% G1's proof then chains: cover-extraction (seam 1) + base case (Half 2) on each
1526	% member + locality glue (Half 1).
1527	% ============================================================================
1528	
1529	\begin{lemma}[Finiteness is local on a finite spanning family]
1530	  \label{lem:module_finite_of_localizationSpan}
1531	  \lean{Module.Finite.of_localizationSpan_finite}
1532	  \mathlibok
1533	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.LocalProperties.Submodule}).}
1534	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, \(N\) an \(R\)-module, and \(t \subseteq R\) a
1535	  finite family with \(\mathrm{span}\,t = \top\) (the \(t\) generate the unit
1536	  ideal). Suppose that for each \(g \in t\) the localization \(N_g\) is a finite
1537	  module over the localization \(R_g\). Then \(N\) is a finite \(R\)-module.
1538	\end{lemma}
1539	
1540	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1541	[Half 1 helper — transfer of localized-module finiteness across models]
1542	  \label{lem:finite_localizedModule_transfer}
1543	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.finite_localizedModule_of_isLocalizedModule}
1544	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, \(S \subseteq R\) a multiplicative submonoid,
1545	  and \(M\) an \(R\)-module. Let \(R_S\) be an \(R\)-algebra that localizes \(R\)
1546	  at \(S\), and let \(N\) be an \(R\)-module carrying a compatible \(R_S\)-module
1547	  structure, together with an \(R\)-linear map \(\varphi : M \to N\) exhibiting
1548	  \(N\) as the localization of \(M\) at \(S\) (\(\mathrm{IsLocalizedModule}\,S\,
1549	  \varphi\)). If \(N\) is a finite \(R_S\)-module, then the canonical localized
1550	  module \(\mathrm{LocalizedModule}\,S\,M\) is a finite module over the canonical
1551	  localization \(\mathrm{Localization}\,S\).
1552	\end{lemma}
1553	
1554	\begin{proof}
1555	  The localization map \(\varphi\) provides an \(R\)-linear isomorphism
1556	  \(e : N \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathrm{LocalizedModule}\,S\,M\) (universal property
1557	  of localized modules), and the algebra localization provides an \(R\)-algebra
1558	  isomorphism \(\psi : R_S \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathrm{Localization}\,S\). These
1559	  are compatible: \(e\) is semilinear over \(\psi\), i.e.\ \(e(a \cdot x) =
1560	  \psi(a) \cdot e(x)\) for \(a \in R_S\), \(x \in N\) (both sides are pinned by
1561	  clearing a common denominator \(s \in S\), where the \(R\)-actions agree). A
1562	  finite \(R_S\)-spanning set \(T\) of \(N\) therefore maps under \(e\) to a
1563	  finite set whose \(\mathrm{Localization}\,S\)-span is all of
1564	  \(\mathrm{LocalizedModule}\,S\,M\); hence the canonical model is finite over
1565	  \(\mathrm{Localization}\,S\).
1566	\end{proof}
1567	
1568	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1569	[Half 1 — G1 locality reduction over a finite basic-open cover]
1570	  \label{lem:gf_finite_sections_of_basicOpen_finite_cover}
1571	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gf_finite_sections_of_basicOpen_finite_cover}
1572	  \uses{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen, lem:finite_localizedModule_transfer,
1573	        lem:module_finite_of_localizationSpan, lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}
1574	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Properties of Schemes", Tag 01PB, proof, gluing step
1575	  % (read from references/stacks-properties.tex, L2107-L2109).
1576	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
1577	  % "Thus $M_{f_i}$ is finitely generated for each $i$. Hence we conclude by
1578	  %  Algebra, Lemma \ref{algebra-lemma-cover}."
1579	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Properties of Schemes", Tag 01PB (gluing step,
1580	  Algebra Lemma \texttt{lemma-cover}).}
1581	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, \(\F\) a quasi-coherent sheaf of modules on \(X\), and
1582	  \(W \subseteq X\) an affine open. Suppose \(t \subseteq \Gamma(X, W)\) is a finite
1583	  family with \(\mathrm{Ideal.span}\,t = \top\) (so the basic opens \(D(g)\),
1584	  \(g \in t\), cover \(W\)), and that for each \(g \in t\) the section module
1585	  \(\Gamma(\F, D(g))\) is finite over \(\Gamma(X, D(g))\). Then \(\Gamma(\F, W)\)
1586	  is a finite \(\Gamma(X, W)\)-module.
1587	\end{lemma}
1588	
1589	\begin{proof}
1590	  \uses{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen, lem:finite_localizedModule_transfer,
1591	        lem:module_finite_of_localizationSpan, lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}
1592	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag 01PB (gluing step).}
1593	  By the local-on-a-spanning-family criterion \cref{lem:module_finite_of_localizationSpan}
1594	  it suffices to show, for each \(g \in t\), that the localization of
1595	  \(\Gamma(\F, W)\) at \(g\) is finite over the localization
1596	  \((\Gamma(X, W))_g\). Fix \(g \in t\). The basic open \(D(g)\) is affine, and the
1597	  restriction \(\Gamma(X, W) \to \Gamma(X, D(g))\) exhibits \(\Gamma(X, D(g))\) as
1598	  the away-localization \((\Gamma(X, W))_g\)
1599	  (\cref{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}); moreover the section restriction
1600	  \(\Gamma(\F, W) \to \Gamma(\F, D(g))\) exhibits \(\Gamma(\F, D(g))\) as the
1601	  localized module \((\mathrm{powers}\,g)^{-1}\,\Gamma(\F, W)\)
1602	  (\cref{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}). Thus \(\Gamma(\F, D(g))\) is a
1603	  model of the localized module, finite over \(\Gamma(X, D(g)) = (\Gamma(X, W))_g\)
1604	  by hypothesis; the model-transfer lemma
1605	  \cref{lem:finite_localizedModule_transfer} carries this finiteness to the
1606	  canonical localized module of \(\Gamma(\F, W)\) at the powers of \(g\), over
1607	  \((\Gamma(X, W))_g\). This supplies the per-element hypothesis, and
1608	  \cref{lem:module_finite_of_localizationSpan} concludes that \(\Gamma(\F, W)\) is
1609	  finite over \(\Gamma(X, W)\).
1610	\end{proof}
